RepeatMD Sales Resource
V1 · Problems · Discovery · War Room · Objections
The frame every call starts from

The modern patient is worth twice as much, and most practices aren’t set up to capture it.

Patient behavior has shifted. Treatments start a decade earlier than the prior generation. Patients spend 20 to 30 percent more through memberships and financing. They refer five times more through social influence. The practices that adapt to this capture dramatically more revenue per patient. The practices that don’t lose them to the ones who did.

Traditional patient
Ages 45 to 75
Avg visit
$550
Visits per year
4
Per year
$2,200
Lifetime value
$66,000
Modern patient
Ages 30 to 75
Avg visit
$350
Visits per year
8
Per year
$2,800
Lifetime value
$126,000
+91%
lifetime value, per patient

EMRs run the back office.
They don’t grow it.

BLVD, Zenoti, Vagaro, Nextech, PatientNow. Every practice has one. None of them are built to drive revenue, retention, or cross-sell. RepeatMD is the growth layer that sits alongside whatever EMR they already use.

Most sales happen
after business hours.

86 percent of in-app purchases happen outside the practice. 35 percent of service appointments are booked between 10pm and 7am. A practice without 24/7 selling is leaving meaningful revenue on the table every night.

86% / 35%

Retention is 7x cheaper
than acquisition.

Loyalty program members visit more frequently. They spend more on visits four through seven than on visits one through three. The growth lever isn’t finding more patients. It’s getting the patients they have to come back.

7x cheaper

Problems by vertical

What each practice type is actually struggling with, and how to position RepeatMD against the specific shape of their pain. Cards covered: med spa, cosmetic dermatology, plastic surgery, health & wellness.

Evidence base: This card is anchored to 11 med spa calls in the war room corpus (Maor, Jennifer, Sara, Shannon, Marcela, Cindy, Tabia, Andy, Vicky, Maria, Ryan). Verbatim quotes below are sourced; click through to the war room tile for the full call context.

Who they are

Solo or small-team med spas with three or more cash-pay service categories. Practice age ranges widely (one of the cleanest splits in the corpus): newer practices under two years old that came in expecting structure they don’t have yet, and established practices three-plus years old that built membership architecture that isn’t producing. Both shapes show up frequently; both close.

What lands: the patient-demand-meeting-broken-architecture frame, the after-hours selling frame, the recurring-revenue frame. Patients are already asking these owners for memberships and a way to buy outside office hours; the practice doesn’t have the infrastructure to deliver. The pitch is operational fix, not concept introduction.

What they actually say on calls

  • “I do have membership, but I have not sold one.” Maria (Rejuvenation Medical Aesthetics)
  • “Bronze, it doesn’t work. It’s just there.” Marcela (Skin Avenue)
  • “It’s been like a year that my clients ask me for memberships, but they don’t have any incentive to go there every month.” Marcela (Skin Avenue)
  • “Everything’s done through Shopify and then Shopify apps… they don’t communicate with each other. Now we have an issue, double-booking.” Marcela (Skin Avenue)
  • “It’s hard to scale it because it’s based off of like the Zenoti platform, and anything that changes, it’s just a pain.” Dr. Ramos (Ageless Health)
  • “We’ve had people do it for six months and then they stop — they get their commitment done and then they stop.” Shannon (Younger Aesthetics)

Four core pain points

01
Existing membership doesn’t convert

21 of 32 calls in the corpus have a membership architecture already in place that isn’t producing. The structural problem isn’t whether to build memberships, it’s why the ones they have aren’t selling. Patients ask for them, owners build them in their current EMR, and they sit unused. Hardest pattern in the corpus and the cleanest wedge for RepeatMD. Position: hands-off member management, visible non-member-vs-member pricing on every transaction, automated billing and dispute handling. The membership doesn’t sell itself in an EMR; it sells itself in an app where the patient sees it on every page.

02
After-hours and weekend demand

Modern patients shop on their phones between 8 and 10 PM, on weekends, on holidays. The front desk is closed. The skin-and-tonic reference case shows $167K of revenue at 8 PM in a year, $120K at 7 PM, $182K at lunch. Practices that can’t sell when patients want to buy lose those transactions to whichever competitor has an app. Position: the white-labeled app with Affirm financing, Apple Pay, and a 24-hour AI treatment advisor captures demand the front desk would otherwise miss.

03
Vendor sprawl and integration failure

Operationally rigorous practices end up managing four to seven separate tools (EMR, booking, marketing, payments, retail, email, SMS) that don’t talk to each other. Marcela’s Shopify-plus-apps stack is producing double-bookings. Tiffany Peninsula was managing Constant Contact plus five separate vendors. Vendor consolidation is now a near-pattern at two instances and growing. Position: the pitch wedge is “here’s how this replaces three or four line items,” not “here’s another great app.” When sprawl is named, run the cost-of-current-stack diagnostic before the standard product walkthrough.

04
Cross-sell stuck at single-service relationships

Patients come for one service (usually injectables) and don’t discover the rest of the menu. Marcela: “I have a lot of injectables patients because we were running Botox campaigns, but it was so hard to keep those patients and sell other things.” The front desk doesn’t have time to educate; the patient doesn’t pick up a brochure. The result is a single-treatment relationship instead of a multi-treatment one. Position: rewards stack toward the services the practice wants to sell more of (memberships, lasers, body contouring). The AI treatment advisor recommends services from the actual menu. Confetti and reward unlocks pull patients into modalities they didn’t come in for.

One-line hooks for openers

  • Your patients have been asking for a membership for a year. The problem isn’t whether to have one; it’s whether the one you have is set up to actually sell.
  • If your patients are shopping at 8 PM and your practice is closed, you’re losing the most valuable hour of the day to whoever has an app.
  • When you’re running Shopify plus four other apps that don’t talk to each other, the problem isn’t adding another tool. It’s replacing the ones that aren’t working.
  • Single-service patients are 25 percent of their lifetime value. Multi-service patients are the other 75. RepeatMD is the bridge.
Evidence gap: No cosmetic dermatology calls in the war room corpus yet. The card below is sketched from the product decks, the ICP doc, and the broader sales motion, but the verbatims and pain rankings aren’t evidence-backed. Treat as a working hypothesis until a derm call comes in.

Who they are

Fragmented vertical. Cosmetic-first practices look more like med spas. Medical-first practices with a cosmetic add-on are a different conversation entirely (and often not a fit). The ones we sell are doctor-led practices with three or more cosmetic services and a real cash-pay revenue line.

They see themselves above the med spa register. The framing has to be premium. They want EMR integration with NextTech or Modernizing Medicine. 90 percent want memberships or retention automation. They don’t want to be sold a “discounting” tool.

What they say on calls

  • “I’m a doctor, not a med spa.”
  • “Does this integrate with NextTech?”
  • “We don’t discount.”
  • “The app looks generic, not custom enough to our brand.”
  • “What can’t we list because of insurance and legal?”

Four core pain points

01
System fragmentation

EMR for clinical, a different system for cosmetic, a third for marketing, a fourth for retention. Nothing talks to anything. The admin burden is real and growing.Position: RepeatMD is the unified patient-facing layer. It complements the EMR, doesn’t replace it.

02
The brand register problem

They won’t use a tool that makes their practice look like a Groupon med spa. Generic discounting language is a non-starter. Membership and premium experience are the right doors.Position: lead with the “Sephora-style app for your practice” framing. Memberships, not discounts. Premium, not promotional.

03
Cosmetic revenue underdevelopment

The cosmetic side of a derm practice often runs at a fraction of its real revenue potential because the clinical side absorbs the operational attention. They know it. They want to fix it without hiring a full cosmetic team.Position: automate cross-sell, after-hours capture, and retention so the cosmetic side scales without proportional staff growth.

04
Practice valuation

Owners thinking about exit, succession, or rolling up know that recurring revenue and digital infrastructure increase practice valuation. Memberships and a branded app are concrete moves toward a higher exit multiple.Position: recurring memberships can increase practice valuation 3 to 5x. The branded app is a durable asset.

One-line hooks for openers

  • RepeatMD isn’t a med spa tool, it’s the layer that lets your cosmetic side scale without growing your staff.
  • Your EMR runs the back office. RepeatMD runs the cosmetic-revenue line.
  • Memberships don’t mean discounts. They mean recurring revenue and a higher exit multiple.
  • If your patients can’t buy from you at 10pm, your cosmetic ceiling is already in.
Evidence base: Nine plastic surgery calls in the war room corpus, now the second-largest vertical after med spa. Four are surgeon-on-call (Kristopher at Pacific Sound, Jigar at Prime Facial, Azar, Bill at Peninsula); five are admin or marketing-director buyers (Tiffany, Kristin, Dana at Gulf Coast, Ivette at Miami, Sandra at London). The surgeon-vs-admin buyer split is real and shapes the objection pattern: surgeons interrogate contracts, competition, and anti-kickback compliance directly; admins focus on cross-pollination and operational fit. Operational shapes are consistent across both: multi-entity bank routing, head-to-head financing comparison, vendor consolidation as a buying criterion, and surgical-to-aesthetic cross-pollination as the core growth goal.

Who they are

Surgical-led practices that have added or want to add a non-surgical menu. Big-ticket procedures, low-volume relationships. They consider themselves above med spas and don’t want to use the same tools. The fit conversation is about expanding into aesthetics, not optimizing what they already do.

Two buyer shapes, and which one you get changes the whole call. When the surgeon is on the call (Kristopher, Jigar, Azar, Bill), they interrogate the product directly — contracts, competition, anti-kickback compliance, fee math — and they decide in the room. When an administrator, practice manager, or marketing director is the buyer (Tiffany, Kristin, Dana, Ivette, Sandra), the surgeon signs but doesn’t attend, so the pitch has to be defensible to an absent stakeholder and the recording has to carry the internal sell. Multi-entity structures are common across both: Tiffany’s Peninsula, Dana’s two entities, and Ivette’s three locations each on their own bank account.

What they actually say on calls

  • “Cherry gives us 2.9 percent at 24 months at zero.” Bill (Peninsula Plastic Surgery)
  • “We have a no long term contract policy that is strict in our practice.” Kristopher (Pacific Sound, surgeon on call)
  • “We’ve got at least 10 different software packages that are each like three, four, 500 dollars a month.” Azar (vendor fatigue)
  • “Because of the Stark laws and the anti-kickback laws, we’ve been very concerned about doing anything aggressive.” Azar (compliance, surgeon-specific)
  • “Each office has its own individual bank account.” Ivette (Miami, multi-entity)

Five core pain points

01
No relationship after surgery

The surgical patient is a one-time relationship by design. The practice spent meaningful CAC to win a single transaction and then loses the patient forever. The non-surgical menu is the answer; the infrastructure to support it isn’t there. Surgical-to-aesthetic cross-pollination is the single most common growth goal across the plastics corpus — Dana named the full funnel (Botox to filler to laser to surgery, plus surgical patients who never enter the med spa), Jigar and Azar both want it in both directions, and Jennifer on the Jigar call named the facial-plastics paradox precisely: do the work well and the patient looks great and feels no reason to return. Position: convert the one-time surgical patient into a recurring non-surgical patient via memberships, packages, and rewards across the non-surgical menu. The app becomes the bridge between surgical recovery and ongoing aesthetic maintenance.

02
Multi-entity bank routing

Plastics practices with both a surgical entity and a non-surgical entity, or multiple locations, need transactions routed correctly so the CPA doesn’t end up reconciling commingled funds. Multi-entity / MSO structures are a load-bearing pattern in plastics: Kristin probed it hard, Tiffany’s Peninsula has three entities, Dana runs two, and Ivette’s Miami practice is the clearest articulation in the corpus — three locations each with its own bank account, which means each needs its own app, not one app with a location dropdown. Position: separate bank accounts require separate RepeatMD instances. The patient logs into each location app with the same phone number, but the apps are distinct because the money flows to distinct accounts. Confirm the bank-account structure at discovery; it determines the architecture. Honest deferral on the operational specifics is the right rep move when sophisticated buyers ask.

03
Financing economics, head-to-head

Cherry, CareCredit, PatientFi, Alphaeon all have specific plans plastics use heavily. The financing conversation is concrete, comparative, and the prospect knows the exact numbers (Bill quoted Cherry’s 2.9 percent at 24 months from memory; Azar runs 20-30 percent of his $10-30K surgical cases through financing). Patient-financing comparison is the heaviest formal pattern in plastics, now around eight instances corpus-wide, and Bill remains the cleanest executive walkthrough. Position: head-to-head comparison. Affirm at 3.99 percent flat regardless of term length, 81 percent approval, $22,500 line. The defensible pitch is “flat-rate practice fee against variable-rate sliding scale,” not “our financing is better.” For surgeons running six figures a year in financing volume, the flat-rate savings is the single strongest economic lever in the pitch.

04
Admin-as-buyer dynamics

When the surgeon doesn’t attend, the Patient Care Coordinator, practice manager, or marketing director is the actual buyer. Now a formal pattern at six instances (Kristin onboarding for her surgeon, Cindy for hers, Tiffany evaluating for Dr. McGregor, Dana the marketing director, Ivette the administrator across three locations, Sandra the administrator pitching her CEO). This shapes the entire call: pricing has to be defensible to an absent stakeholder, workflow has to be explainable to staff who didn’t see the demo, and the buyer often becomes the internal champion (Sandra explicitly pitching RepeatMD over billboards and radio to her CEO). Position: ship the recording proactively — it carries the internal sell. Arm the champion with the comparison math they need. Don’t push card-on-call; respect the chain.

05
Surgeon-on-call: a different objection shape

When the surgeon is on the call, the dynamic flips. Now a formal pattern at four instances (Kristopher, Jigar, Azar, Bill), cross-rep across Remy, Taylor, and Nellie. Surgeons interrogate the product directly and decide in the room: Kristopher ran the competition probe as a live Google search and named a strict no-long-term-contract policy from prior vendor burns; Azar raised Stark and anti-kickback compliance head-on and audited his 10-plus software subscriptions for overlap; Bill named the ASAPS peer-check as his gate. The objections are sharper and more skeptical than the admin pattern, but the close is faster when handled right — Kristopher went from contract objection to card-on-call in about two minutes once the 90-day addendum was structured around his policy. Position: don’t fight the surgeon’s skepticism, structure around it. The 90-day addendum (written, leadership named, overriding the standard 12-month DocuSign language) is the answer to the no-contract objection. Lead with the financing fee math and consolidation savings, not the feature tour. Surgeons trust other surgeons — respect peer-validation gates.

One-line hooks for openers

  • The surgical patient is a one-time transaction. The non-surgical patient is a lifetime relationship. RepeatMD is how you convert one into the other.
  • RepeatMD doesn’t compete with Allē; it stacks on top of it.
  • If you have separate bank accounts per location or entity, the architecture is separate apps — one per account. Tell me how your money flows and I’ll tell you how it’s built.
  • If you’re paying eight percent on Cherry and approval is sixty percent, we should look at the head-to-head math.
  • No long-term contract. Ninety-day addendum, then month-to-month. The structure exists because surgeons told us they won’t sign a year.
  • The referral reward can be configured receiver-only, which keeps it compliant. Anything beyond that is a question for your counsel, not for me.
Evidence base: Twelve wellness calls in the war room corpus — the original four (Marie, April, Daniel, Mike Reno at BWell) plus eight from the wellness expansion (Don/Renew, Luiz/Allura, Jennifer/Superior, Sultana/Be The Change, Teresa/Integrity, Nicole/Blue River, Nik/Primary Choice, Sheena/Sheena Rose). Includes the wellness-bank-membership reference case (Marie), the most complex multi-modality build (BWell, 70/30 hybrid), the cohort’s only closed deal (Nik, signed and paid on the call), and the clearest declined-on-affordability call (Jennifer/Superior).

Who they are

Broad category. IV therapy clinics, integrative medicine practices, weight-loss-focused wellness centers, longevity clinics. The fit varies enormously. Standalone IV bars look different from full wellness practices with semaglutide, peptides, hormone therapy, and aesthetic add-ons.

Most are newer (one to five years) and founder-as-provider, but established multi-modality wellness practices show up too (Marie’s 17-year-old primary practice expanding into a second location, BWell at multi-modality 70/30 split between wellness and aesthetic). The big trend driving inbound is GLP-1s, longevity, and peptides. Patient leakage to online compounders and telehealth brands is now the defining wellness-vertical pain — a formal pattern at seven instances across three reps. The expansion cohort is overwhelmingly solo or tiny-team providers moving from insurance toward cash-pay, a distinct buyer from the med spa and plastics corpus.

What they actually say on calls

  • “We’re losing patients to online compounders for the GLP-1s.” composite (7 calls: BWell, Daniel, Jennifer/Superior, Sultana, Nicole, Nik, Sheena)
  • “The peptide intake form workflow is what I need to understand.” Daniel (longevity practice)
  • “We have a wellness side and an aesthetic side and the patient base doesn’t cross over the way I want.” Mike Reno (BWell)
  • “I want to do a membership but I can’t figure out how to make it work across these different service frequencies.” composite (Marie + April)
  • “Are you guys going to be a Vagaro replacement or do we keep both?” composite (multiple wellness calls)

Four core pain points

01
Patient leakage to online compounders

GLP-1 patients are buying semaglutide and tirzepatide from online prescribers, compounding pharmacies, and telehealth brands (Hims, Hers, NewSelf) at $79-100/month. The practice loses the patient entirely once they switch. Now a firmly formal pattern at seven instances across three reps — the single most important wellness-vertical retention problem. Position: lock the patient into a wellness-bank membership where the dollars stay in the practice. Banking memberships convert price-sensitivity into commitment. The patient is buying $200 of credit, not $200 of GLP-1, which means they come back for the rest of the service menu too.

02
Membership structure mismatch

Wellness service frequencies don’t map cleanly to monthly memberships the way injectables do. IV therapy might be weekly, peptides monthly, hormone testing quarterly. Marie’s call surfaced this directly: the generic girl-math framing didn’t land because her actual service frequencies didn’t support a monthly cadence. Position: the wellness-bank membership (Misha-style banking architecture) is the structural answer for wellness frequency mismatch. Patient commits to a monthly dollar amount, draws against it at whatever cadence makes sense for their treatment plan.

03
Multi-modality patient handoff

Multi-modality wellness practices (BWell, Mike Reno’s 70/30 split) have wellness patients who never cross over to aesthetics and aesthetic patients who never cross over to wellness. The cross-modality referral is broken at the front desk. Position: rewards stack across modalities. Wellness patients get aesthetic-side rewards, aesthetic patients get wellness-side rewards, AI advisor recommends services from the entire menu regardless of how the patient came in.

04
Tool overlap anxiety

Wellness practices have often patched together five tools that almost do what they want and are reasonably worried about adding a sixth that overlaps. The vendor-consolidation framing surfaced repeatedly in wellness discovery. Position: be specific about what RepeatMD does and doesn’t replace. The patient-facing experience, the rewards engine, the financing layer, the membership management. Not a replacement for booking or EMR. When sprawl is named, run the cost-of-current-stack diagnostic.

One-line hooks for openers

  • Your GLP-1 patients are price-shopping online compounders. The wellness-bank membership is how you convert price-sensitivity into a year of revenue.
  • Your patients are buying their supplements somewhere. Today it’s Amazon. We’ll make it you.
  • Memberships are the difference between a wellness practice and a wellness business. The structure for wellness is different from injectables — banking, not monthly service.
  • If your wellness side and your aesthetic side feel like two different practices, the cross-modality reward engine is the bridge.
Internal working tool. Sources: WIP deck, Sales Training deck, Discovery deck, Responsible Value-Based Selling deck, ICP doc, Needs/Objections/Value/Personas doc. Med spa vertical drafted from product material pending Mike’s validation.

Find the lever that matters for this practice. Demo the lever. Close the lever.

Discovery is how you figure out which of the six revenue levers RepeatMD pulls is the one this practice actually needs. Generic discovery wastes the call. Get the right lever named in the first fifteen minutes, then the demo and the close are downhill. The questions below are organized by vertical because what plastics owners care about is not what wellness founders care about. Use them as threads to pull, not checklists to run.

The six levers, every call is one of these
Loyalty & Rewards
cross-sell, first-time return
Memberships
recurring revenue, valuation
Referrals
CAC drop, social proof
Financing
ticket size, approval rate
Med-Commerce
after-hours, retail, gift cards
Operations
automation, reporting, promos

Discovery by vertical

Each vertical has the questions to ask, the threads to pull, and what to listen for in the answers. Compressed to high-signal questions only. Encyclopedic version lives downstream once the War Room call evidence fills in.

Evidence base: Med spa discovery is now anchored to 11 corpus calls. The thread structure below is the working set of questions; the “Listen for” notes are updated with the actual frequency of each response shape in the war room evidence.
Warm start (always first)

“Before I get into anything, can I learn a bit about your practice?”

Get the shape: number of locations, providers on staff, top five services by revenue, how long they’ve been open. Mix question matters: what percent of revenue is procedures vs retail, and what’s the cash-pay split. Listen for tox-and-filler-heavy vs broad-menu, single-location vs multi-location. The discovery you run next bends to what you hear here.

Loyalty & rewards · cross-sell

“What percent of your Botox patients have ever tried any of your other services?”

  • If they don’t know the number: “Roughly. Half? Less than a quarter?”
  • “Which service do you wish patients tried earlier in their journey?”
  • “What’s the biggest missed cross-sell sitting on your menu right now?”
  • “If we could spotlight three services in-app for every new patient, which three would you pick?”
Listen forfrustration that the menu is broad but utilization is narrow. That’s the cross-sell lever talking.
Memberships · recurring revenue

“Do you have a membership program today?”

  • If yes: “What’s the price point, how many active members, and what’s your monthly churn?”
  • If yes: “What’s the manual lift on your team every month to run it?”
  • If no: “What stopped you, was it ops, pricing, tech, or marketing?”
  • “What recurring revenue number per month would actually move the needle for you?”
  • “If your member base doubled in six months, what changes in how you run the practice?”
Listen forexisting-but-broken architecture is the highest-probability answer in the corpus (21 of 32 calls). Patients are asking for memberships, owners built them in their EMR or Shopify, and they sit unused. The wedge isn’t whether to build memberships, it’s why the ones they have aren’t converting. Sample verbatims to expect: “I have membership but I have not sold one” (Maria), “Bronze, it doesn’t work, it’s just there” (Marcela), “people do it for six months and then they stop” (Shannon).
Med-Commerce · after-hours

“How does your front desk handle the booking inquiries that come in after you’re closed?”

  • “How many inquiries come in overnight or on weekends, roughly?”
  • “What percent of those convert into actual bookings?”
  • “Can patients buy a package or gift card from you right now without talking to anyone?”
  • “What bundles or packages do you wish were live online today?”
Listen forthey know about the after-hours demand. They’re losing it. They don’t have a tool that captures it.
Operations · promo ROI

“Walk me through what happens after a promo runs. How do you measure what worked?”

  • “Who builds the promos, who pushes them out, who tracks the results?”
  • “What reports do you actually look at every week?”
  • “If you ran the same promo twice, would you know which time worked better and why?”
Listen forvague answers. The ROI blind spot is real and they know it; they just don’t have the instrumentation.
Operations · vendor consolidation

“Walk me through what tools you’re running today — EMR, booking, marketing, payments, retail, email.”

  • “How many of those talk to each other, and how many are you reconciling manually?”
  • “Where are the failure points — double bookings, missed leads, billing mismatches?”
  • “What does the monthly cost of all of those add up to right now?”
  • “If you could collapse half of them into one tool, which half would you drop first?”
Listen forvendor sprawl is now a near-pattern (Marcela + Tiffany, two of the most operationally rigorous buyers in the corpus). When sprawl is named, the pitch wedge becomes “here’s how this replaces three or four line items,” not “here’s another great app.” Marcela’s articulation is the cleanest reference: Shopify plus multiple apps producing double-bookings. Run the cost-of-current-stack diagnostic before the standard product walkthrough.
Financing · ticket size

“What are you using for patient financing today, and what fee are you paying?”

  • “What’s your approval rate on financing requests in the last 30 days?”
  • “How often are patients walking away from a recommended treatment because of the cost?”
  • “What services would unlock if patients could finance over 36 months instead of 24?”
Listen forspecific dollar fees they’re paying. That sets up the head-to-head Affirm comparison in the demo.
Cost of status quo, for the away-buyer bridge

If nothing changes, where does the practice land?

Ask these when the prospect has named pain but hasn’t quantified what it’s costing them. The job is to make the cost real before pivoting to the solution. The questions below are calibrated to the most common pain shapes in the corpus.

  • You said patients have been asking for memberships. If that demand stays unmet for another twelve months, how many of those patients will quietly switch to a competitor who has the program?
  • You named the after-hours window. Skin-and-tonic does $167K at 8 PM in a year. If you’re open three years from now without an app, what fraction of that revenue are you choosing not to capture?
  • You’re paying for [Shopify / Vagaro / GoHighLevel / Constant Contact] today. What’s the monthly run rate of the tools you’re reconciling manually, and what would consolidating two or three of them free up for the practice?
  • How many Botox-only patients walked out last quarter who should have left as multi-service buyers? At your average ticket, what does that gap add up to in twelve months?
Recap script, before the demo

“From what you’ve shared, your top goals are __ and __. The blockers are __ and __. If we solved those, you’d expect __ within 90 days. Did I get that right?”

Read it back in their own words, not yours. Wait for the “yes that’s right.” That yes is the permission to demo. If they correct you on anything, demo into the corrected version.

Evidence gap: No cosmetic dermatology calls in the war room corpus yet. The threads below are sketched from the product decks, ICP doc, and the derm-specific framings in the broader sales motion. Treat as a working hypothesis until a derm call comes in.
Warm start (always first)

“Tell me about how the cosmetic side of your practice runs today.”

Establish the shape first: medical vs cosmetic revenue split, which EMR they use, do they have a dedicated cosmetic team or shared staff, what cash-pay services they actively promote. Listen for the doctor-not-a-medspa register. Match the tone. Memberships and premium experience, never discounts.

Operations · EMR integration

“Which EMR are you on, and what’s working and not working about how it handles the cosmetic side?”

  • “Does it handle membership tracking, or are you doing that elsewhere?”
  • “What patient-facing tools do you have today, and what’s missing?”
  • “What’s the gap between what your EMR does and what your patients actually expect from you?”
Listen forNextTech and Modernizing Medicine come up most. They want to know we complement, not replace. Name the complementary positioning early.
Memberships · valuation

“Have you thought about memberships as a way to build recurring revenue on the cosmetic side?”

  • “What’s your long-term plan for the practice, is exit or expansion on the horizon?”
  • “Do you think about practice valuation when you think about your service mix?”
  • “What would member-only pricing or perks let you do that you can’t do with a standard menu?”
Listen forowners thinking about exit or expansion. Memberships as a valuation multiplier is the doctor-friendly framing, not the discounting framing.
Loyalty & rewards · cosmetic underdevelopment

“What percent of your cosmetic patients have tried more than one of your treatments?”

  • “Where does cross-sell actually happen today, at consult, in the chair, in follow-up?”
  • “What’s the next treatment you wish more of your Botox patients knew about?”
  • “Is the clinical staff doing cosmetic education, or is it a separate conversation?”
Listen forderm-specific friction: the clinical workflow doesn’t naturally introduce the cosmetic menu. AI advisors solve that without the doctor having to recommend.
Med-Commerce · brand register

“If your patients could shop your cosmetic menu from their phone, what would feel right to your brand and what wouldn’t?”

  • “What does your brand stand for that a generic loyalty app would get wrong?”
  • “Where’s the line for you between premium and promotional?”
Listen forbrand sensitivity. The Sephora-style framing lands here; the Groupon-style framing kills the deal. Mirror their register.
Operations · insurance and legal

“Are there services on your cosmetic menu you’d hesitate to put in a patient-facing app for insurance or legal reasons?”

  • “What’s gray area for you, vs what’s clearly fine?”
Listen forderm-specific concern that doesn’t show up in med spa calls. Acknowledge directly, talk about what we list and don’t list. Don’t handwave.
Cost of status quo, for the away-buyer bridge

If the cosmetic side stays where it is, what does that cost?

Derm owners often have a strong sense the cosmetic side is underperforming relative to its real ceiling. The job is to put a number on that gap.

  • If cosmetic revenue stayed flat for the next year, would the practice hit its targets?
  • How does practice valuation look without a recurring revenue line?
  • What does losing a cosmetic patient to a med spa across town actually cost you?
  • If you can’t grow cosmetic without hiring, what’s the headcount math?
Recap script, before the demo

“What I’m hearing is the cosmetic side has room to grow but you don’t want to scale by adding staff or discounting. You want a premium patient experience that complements your EMR and builds recurring revenue. Is that right?”

The premium-not-promotional framing matters. If they nod on that exact phrasing, the demo can be more confident. If they hesitate, dig before showing.

Evidence base: Nine plastic surgery calls in the corpus, now the second-largest vertical. Four surgeon-on-call (Kristopher at Pacific Sound, Jigar at Prime Facial, Azar, Bill at Peninsula) and five admin or marketing-director buyers (Tiffany, Kristin, Dana at Gulf Coast, Ivette at Miami, Sandra at London). Operational shapes are consistent: surgeon-vs-admin buyer split, multi-entity bank routing (separate accounts can require separate apps), head-to-head financing comparison, vendor consolidation as a buying criterion, anti-kickback compliance as a surgeon-specific objection, and surgical-to-aesthetic cross-pollination as the recurring growth goal.
Warm start (always first)

“Tell me about how your non-surgical menu fits alongside the surgical side.”

Critical to establish: how big is the non-surgical menu, how separated are the workflows, who runs the non-surgical side day-to-day, is the surgeon the bottleneck or the Patient Care Coordinator. The whole pitch is converting one-time surgical patients into recurring non-surgical patients. Confirm that’s the strategic ambition before going further.

Memberships · post-surgical retention

“What happens to a patient after a surgical procedure is done? Do you see them again?”

  • “How often does a surgical patient become a non-surgical patient?”
  • “What’s the average gap between surgery and the next visit?”
  • “Would a maintenance membership tied to their surgery make sense for your patients?”
Listen forthe “we don’t need to see them again” reflex. That’s the assumption to challenge. The surgical patient is a one-time transaction; the non-surgical patient is a lifetime relationship.
Financing · the head-to-head

“What financing options are you offering today, and which ones are your patients actually using?”

  • “Are you on Cherry, CareCredit, PatientFi, all three?”
  • “What practice fee are you paying on each?”
  • “What’s the approval rate looking like, and what does a typical declined patient do next?”
  • “If patients could finance over 36 months instead of 24, what would change?”
Listen forspecifics. Plastics will name fees and approvals confidently — Bill at Peninsula quoted Cherry’s 2.9% at 24 months at zero from memory mid-call, and Azar runs 20-30% of his $10-30K surgical cases through financing. Patient-financing comparison is the heaviest pattern in plastics, now around eight instances corpus-wide. That sets up the comparison: 3.99% flat regardless of term, 81% approval, soft credit, $22,500 line, 36-month term. The defensible pitch is “flat-rate practice fee vs variable-rate sliding scale,” not “our financing is better.” For a surgeon doing six figures a year in financing volume, the flat-rate savings is the single strongest economic lever.
Loyalty & rewards · the Allē/Aspire question

“Your patients are probably already enrolled in Allē or Aspire. How are you thinking about RepeatMD alongside that?”

  • “Do you feel like the manufacturer programs are driving practice-level loyalty, or just product-level loyalty?”
  • “Where do you want a patient’s loyalty to point, to Allergan, or to your practice?”
Listen forfrustration that manufacturer programs reward the manufacturer, not the practice. The question usually comes from the provider, not the administrator — Maylen, the NP on the Ivette call, asked how the membership integrates with patients’ existing Allē points. The answer is the double-dip plus the brand-ownership reframe: patients keep Allē and get the practice’s own branded loyalty on top, and Allē can be linked inside the app’s discover tab. RepeatMD stacks on top, practice-level. Name that clearly — the practice owns its patient relationship rather than handing it to Allergan.
Operations · the PCC and consult workflow

“Walk me through what a Patient Care Coordinator does from first inquiry to scheduled surgery.”

  • “How much time per patient is spent on FAQs, scheduling, follow-up?”
  • “What questions do patients ask that you wish you could answer 24/7 without a human?”
  • “Where does the workflow break down most often?”
Listen forPCC overload, and notice who’s actually on the call — it determines everything. Admin-as-buyer-on-behalf-of-surgeon is now a formal pattern at six instances (Kristin, Cindy, and Tiffany onboarding for their surgeons; Dana the marketing director; Ivette the administrator across three locations; Sandra the administrator pitching her CEO). When the surgeon isn’t in the room, pricing has to be defensible to an absent stakeholder, workflow has to be explainable to staff who didn’t see the demo, and the buyer often becomes the internal champion. AI Treatment Advisors handle the FAQs and pre-consult education, freeing the PCC for high-value conversion. Ship the recording proactively — it carries the internal sell. Don’t push card-on-call; respect the chain. (When the surgeon is on the call, the dynamic flips entirely — see the surgeon-on-call thread below.)
Operations · multi-entity bank routing

“Do you have separate legal entities for the surgical side and the non-surgical side, or is it one EIN?”

  • “If separate, how do you currently route revenue and reconcile between them?”
  • “Does your CPA prefer the two sides stay operationally separate, or does it not matter to them?”
  • “Has commingled funds ever been an issue you’ve had to clean up?”
Listen formulti-entity / MSO structures, a load-bearing pattern in plastics now around six instances. Kristin probed it hard; Tiffany’s Peninsula has three entities; Dana runs two; and Ivette’s Miami practice is the clearest articulation in the corpus — three locations each with its own bank account, which means each needs its own app. The rule: separate bank accounts require separate RepeatMD instances. The patient logs into each location app with the same phone number, but the apps are distinct because the money flows to distinct accounts. Ask how the money flows before you describe the architecture. Honest deferral on operational specifics is the right move when sophisticated buyers ask — a signature execution pattern now at six instances (Daniel, Kristin, Tiffany, Andy, plus Remy’s Kristopher and Jigar surgeon closes).
Buyer dynamics · surgeon on the call

“You’ve clearly looked at a lot of vendors. What’s made you say no to the ones you’ve turned down?”

  • “What’s your experience been with long-term contracts? Any that didn’t work out?”
  • “How many software subscriptions are you carrying right now, and where’s the overlap?”
  • “What would you need to see in the first ninety days to know this is working?”
Listen forwhen the surgeon is physically on the call, the whole dynamic flips. Surgeon-on-call is now a formal pattern at four instances (Kristopher, Jigar, Azar, Bill), cross-rep across Remy, Taylor, and Nellie. Surgeons interrogate directly and decide in the room: Kristopher ran the competition probe as a live Google search and named a strict no-long-term-contract policy from prior vendor burns; Azar audited 10-plus software subscriptions for overlap and raised compliance head-on; Bill named the ASAPS peer-check as his gate. The objections are sharper than the admin pattern but the close is faster when handled right — don’t fight the skepticism, structure around it. The 90-day addendum (written, leadership named, overriding the standard 12-month DocuSign language) is the answer to the no-contract objection, and it took Kristopher about two minutes from objection to card-on-call. Lead with financing fee math and consolidation savings, not the feature tour. Respect peer-validation gates — surgeons trust other surgeons.
Compliance · anti-kickback / Stark (near-pattern, plastics/derm-specific)

“How are you handling referral incentives today, given the compliance environment?”

  • “Do you have a current referral program, and how is it structured?”
  • “Has compliance ever held you back from being more aggressive on referrals?”
Listen forplastics and derm surgeons raise anti-kickback and Stark concerns that never appear in the med spa corpus — Azar named it directly and Kristopher was compliance-cautious adjacent (two instances, near-pattern, plastics/derm-only). The correct rep posture is narrow and firm: the referral reward can be configured receiver-only (an introductory offer to the new patient, no kickback to the referrer), and that is the compliant configuration RepeatMD sets up. Anything beyond that — automated sender kickbacks, manual back-end crediting, points workarounds — is a question for the practice’s own counsel, not for the rep. Never characterize a non-compliant approach as acceptable because it is “harder to prove.” Confirm the compliant config and defer the rest to counsel.
Med-Commerce · pricing transparency tension

“How do you think about pricing transparency? What gets quoted publicly vs in consultation?”

  • “What can go in an app, and what stays in the consultation room?”
  • “Are there packages or non-surgical bundles that should be public?”
Listen forplastics-specific sensitivity. Don’t push transparency. The non-surgical menu is where transparency is fine; surgical procedures stay quoted in consultation. Respect the boundary explicitly.
Cost of status quo, for the away-buyer bridge

What does losing the surgical patient cost the practice over a decade?

Plastics owners are big-ticket thinkers. The cost of status quo math here is about LTV per patient over a 10-year horizon, not month-over-month revenue. Questions below calibrated to the operational shapes observed across the plastics corpus — the surgeon-on-call calls (Kristopher, Jigar, Azar, Bill) and the admin-led calls (Dana, Ivette, Sandra, Tiffany, Kristin).

  • How many post-surgical patients did you have last year that never came back for non-surgical maintenance? At average non-surgical ticket, what does that gap add up to in five years?
  • You named the Cherry / CareCredit fee at [X%]. At your current financing volume, what does the delta to 3.99% flat work out to monthly?
  • If your PCC is spending [N] hours per week on consult FAQs that an AI advisor could handle 24/7, what would freeing those hours for high-value conversions actually look like in booked surgeries?
  • If you have separate EINs and your team is reconciling commingled transactions manually, what does the CPA cleanup cost per quarter, and what does eliminating it free up?
Recap script, before the demo

“What I’m hearing is the strategic move is expanding non-surgical revenue without it interfering with the surgical workflow, and financing economics matter a lot. The big unlock is converting one-time surgical patients into recurring non-surgical ones. Right?”

The “without interfering with the surgical workflow” is the credibility line. Plastics owners are protective of their surgical operations. Name it before they ask.

Evidence base: Twelve wellness calls in the war room corpus (the original four plus eight from the wellness expansion: Don, Luiz, Jennifer/Superior, Sultana, Teresa, Nicole, Nik, Sheena). Vertical is heterogeneous — standalone IV bars, multi-modality wellness with aesthetic add-ons, GLP-1-focused weight-loss practices, and longevity clinics each shape the answers differently. Establish fit on the warm start before running the threads below.
Warm start (always first)

“Walk me through your service menu and where most of your revenue actually comes from.”

Establish fit fast: standalone IV vs broader wellness practice, is semaglutide or GLP-1 on the menu, how long the practice has been open, is the founder the provider, are there aesthetic services or just wellness. The discovery thread bends hard based on whether they’re a one-product IV bar or a full multi-service wellness practice.

Memberships · GLP-1 packaging

“If GLP-1s or weight loss are on your menu, how are you structuring them today, monthly, per dose, package?”

  • “What’s your average commitment length, one month, three, longer?”
  • “What percent of weight-loss patients are paying upfront vs financing?”
  • “What does the patient experience look like between visits?”
Listen forper-dose pricing without longer commitment, and patient leakage to online compounders. That leakage is now a firmly formal pattern at seven instances across three reps (BWell, Daniel, Jennifer/Superior, Sultana, Nicole, Nik, Sheena) and still growing as GLP-1 compounding pharmacies and telehealth brands multiply. The lever isn’t just longer packages, it’s wellness-bank memberships that lock in dollars rather than dose-volume. Banking converts price-sensitivity into commitment — the patient is buying $200 of credit, not $200 of GLP-1, which means they come back for the rest of the menu.
Med-Commerce · retail aspiration

“Are you selling retail products today, supplements, skincare, anything physical?”

  • If yes: “What’s working, what’s sitting on shelves?”
  • If no: “Is that something you’ve wanted to do but couldn’t pull the trigger on?”
  • “Where do your patients buy their supplements today?”
  • “Would you sell retail if you didn’t have to carry inventory or handle shipping?”
Listen forthe retail aspiration with no infrastructure. SkinDrop is the answer. Zero inventory, 3,000-plus products, 30% margin, no shipping.
Memberships · the wellness twist

“Have you tried selling memberships, and if so, what stopped them from working?”

  • “What’s the benefit that would actually make a member feel like a member?”
  • “If you had monthly recurring revenue equal to 30% of your current revenue, what changes?”
Listen forfrequency mismatch is the wellness-specific membership problem. Marie’s call surfaced it directly — her actual service frequencies didn’t support a monthly cadence, so the generic girl-math framing didn’t land. The wellness-bank membership (Misha-style banking architecture) is the structural answer: patient commits to a monthly dollar amount and draws against it at whatever cadence makes sense for their treatment plan. Standalone IV practices struggle with traditional memberships because the service is too narrow; banking works for them too. Multi-modality wellness practices like BWell can run both a traditional tier and a banking tier in parallel.
Operations · tool overlap

“What tools are you running today, booking, marketing, financing, payments?”

  • “Vagaro, Square, GoHighLevel, CareCredit, MindBody, which of those?”
  • “Where does your stack work well and where does it break down?”
  • “What would have to be true for adding another tool to feel worth it?”
Listen forwellness practices are tool-fatigued. Acknowledge it directly. Be specific about what RepeatMD does that the existing stack doesn’t. Patient-facing experience, rewards engine, financing layer, membership management. Not booking, not EMR. When the prospect names sprawl explicitly, run the cost-of-current-stack diagnostic before the standard product walkthrough.
Loyalty & rewards · multi-modality cross-pollination

“If you have both a wellness side and an aesthetic side, how often does a wellness patient cross over to aesthetics, or vice versa?”

  • “Which side drives the higher LTV today, and which side has the bigger ceiling?”
  • “Where does the cross-modality handoff break down — at the front desk, in the consult, after a visit?”
  • “If your wellness patients could see and unlock aesthetic-side rewards, would that change which services they ask about?”
Listen formulti-modality practices like BWell (70/30 wellness/aesthetic split) have wellness patients who never cross to aesthetics and vice versa. The cross-modality referral is broken at the front desk. Rewards stack across modalities — wellness patients get aesthetic-side incentives, aesthetic patients get wellness-side ones, AI advisor recommends services from the full menu regardless of how the patient came in. Worth probing the actual cross-over rate as a sizing question (Mike Reno at BWell didn’t articulate this number on his call; it’s a coaching gap worth filling next time).
Financing · the cost-sensitive customer

“How often do you lose a patient at the point of pricing on a higher-ticket package?”

  • “What’s the average package size that hits price resistance?”
  • “What financing are you offering today, what fee, what approval rate?”
  • “Would 36-month financing change what you could sell?”
Listen forprice sensitivity is high in wellness, especially newer practices. The Affirm comparison is real value. Make the math concrete.
Cost of status quo, for the away-buyer bridge

Newer practice, cost-sensitive, what does standing still cost?

Wellness practices often push back on cost because they’re newer or smaller. The CSQ math has to be tight and concrete. Questions below calibrated to the GLP-1 leakage, frequency-mismatch, solo-provider affordability, and multi-modality patterns observed across the twelve corpus calls.

  • You said your GLP-1 patients are starting to ask about online compounders. If 30% of those switch this year, what does that revenue look like against the cost of building a wellness-bank membership now?
  • Your service frequencies don’t map cleanly to a monthly membership. Without a banking structure, what does another twelve months of patients not committing to recurring revenue cost you in cash flow predictability?
  • If your wellness and aesthetic sides have, say, a 10% cross-over rate today, what would a 25% cross-over rate produce in incremental annual revenue per shared patient?
  • You’re paying for booking, marketing, payments, retail, and email separately today. What’s the monthly run rate of the stack, and what would replacing three of those line items free up?
Recap script, before the demo

“Sounds like the priorities are __ and __, and the things slowing you down are __ and __. If we could solve those without adding to your tech stack burden, what would that be worth to you over the next six months?”

The “without adding to your tech stack burden” line is the credibility move for wellness. They’ve been burned by tool-overlap before. Name it explicitly.

Discovery V1. Sources: Discovery deck, Sales Training deck, Responsible Value-Based Selling deck, ICP doc, Needs/Objections/Value/Personas doc. Threads will refine as Gong call evidence lands in the War Room. Med spa and wellness threads carry the heaviest gap-flag weight pending real call data.

A flow, not a script. Adapt to the practice in front of you, don’t recite.

End-to-end playbook for RepeatMD demo calls, built from what has actually worked across the thirty-two calls in the War Room. A script assumes every prospect is the same and runs the same steps in the same order. A flow gives you the spine plus the branches for when the practice in front of you isn’t the default shape. The best calls in the corpus don’t recite the deck. They adapt in the first five minutes based on what they’re hearing, demo the lever that matters, and close with a handful of reusable moves.

The call, end to end

Seven sections, in order. The deck gives you the canonical sequence and language; the War Room tells you what happens when you actually run it. Both are here. Tap through.

This is a call flow, not a script. The deck has a fixed slide order; the calls that close don’t follow it rigidly. They find the one revenue lever the practice actually needs, demo that lever, and close it. Use the deck for the spine and the language, and use the seven sections below to know where the real calls adapt, drill, and close.
The seven sections of every call
1
Before the call — five minutes of prep
Scan the website, socials, and the RMD database if they’re an existing or referred practice. Identify the archetype. Prepare one specific opener tied to the research. Five minutes of prep is the difference between generic and specific, and prospects can tell which one they’re getting.
2
Discovery — find the lever that matters
Where calls are won or lost. Six archetype tracks set your opener and drill sequence. A handful of universal drill moves apply on every call. The deck’s discovery line (“what are the biggest challenges you’ve found in growing your practice?”) is the opener; the drills are how you get past the first answer to the real one. Bridge to the demo when their story is complete, not when your list is.
3
Demo — the patient app, mapped to their lever
The eighteen-step app walkthrough: Home, Rewards, Discover, Shop (gift cards, products, memberships, treatments, packages), Affirm financing, the Adonis AI advisor, Skin Drop, then Admin. Configure to their menu before you share the screen. Reference what they told you in discovery as you go.
4
Admin & back office
The owner-facing dashboard, reporting, promotions engine, two-way messaging, and the EMR/booking integration pause. This is where you answer “who runs this day to day” and “does it replace my current stack.”
5
Pricing
Anchor at standard ($6,997 build, $999 Executive), present the first-call offer ($1,049 build, $799/mo), and hold one deeper lever in reserve ($500 build, $699/mo, 50% off the first month). Three packages: Lite, Professional, Executive. The “you’d pay $10K/month to build this yourself” framing justifies the anchor before you drop it.
6
The close — including the trailblazer reframe
Card on the call is the gold standard. The trailblazer reframe is the timing-objection answer. DocuSign in session converts a verbal yes into a closed deal. Financing-access-via-umbrella unlocks practices that Cherry declined. Reciprocal follow-up is the backup when they genuinely can’t close live.
7
Objections
The recurring objections, each with the answer the corpus shows actually working: timing / just-bought-a-device, affordability, existing-but-broken membership, financing comparison, “another vendor / I have too many tools,” and the no-contract concern.
What this assumes about you
You know the product. You’ve watched a few of the Gong recordings in the War Room. You’re willing to get specific instead of generic. You can sit with silence after you ask a question. Discovery is a skill you’re developing, not a checklist you’re completing.
The slide-by-slide presentation flow, in order, with the talk-track and the question to ask on each slide. This is the front half of the call — the narrative that takes a prospect from their stated problem to wanting the demo. Follow it in sequence, but stay a flow, not a script: mirror their exact language back, skip what doesn’t apply, and bridge to the live demo (the next section) when they’re leaning in. Slide numbers match the deck.
Open — frame the conversation around them
Discovery
4 Built from thousands of practices
“We built this tool with features that solve the most common problems we’ve heard from talking to thousands of practice owners just like you. I want to make sure that when I show it to you, I show you the features that can help you specifically.”

Then the discovery question that opens the whole call: “What are some of the biggest challenges you’ve found in growing your practice today?” Let them answer fully — this is the language you’ll mirror back on slide 15 and aim the demo at.
Endless Ways
5 There are endless ways to spend money on growth
“What we’ve found is that when practice owners look to solve problems and grow, they typically all do the same things. To help with [their problem], for example, you might look to [the most relevant points on the slide]. There are literally endless ways to spend money trying to grow your business.”

The turn: “The problem is they’re all missing one key element — a focus on the patient. Specifically, the modern patient.”
Modern Patient
6 The modern patient is constantly evolving
“The modern patient is constantly evolving. Their preferences are changing, and most businesses haven’t changed to take advantage of it, because they don’t know the data behind it. Many practices have closed because of it. So what I’m about to show you will give you some insight.”
Modern Patient
8 Your ideal patient
“Let me ask you this. When you think about your ideal patient — the one who values your expertise, follows through, and refers others — isn’t that exactly the type of person you’d want more of walking through your doors?”

A soft yes here sets up everything that follows.
Build the market case — momentum and pressure
Investors
9 Big money has noticed
“And big money has noticed. These headlines from the last 18 months — and there are more — represent over a billion dollars flooding into this space, all to attract these exact patients.”
Competition
10 The boom brought competition
“Since COVID, everyone has noticed the boom of this space, which has led to a massive increase in competition.”

Question: “If you’re like most providers I talk to, it feels like everyone and their cousin is starting a clinic. Have you seen a spike in new competitors opening up nearby?”
Expectations
11 Patient expectations are accelerating
“On top of rising competition, the pace of change is only accelerating. Today’s patient has higher expectations than ever. They don’t just want a provider — they expect [mention a few].”
Mirror their problem, then reveal the solution
Solves Problems
15 We solve problems like the one you named
“We solve core problems just like the one you mentioned earlier — [insert their exact phrasing, for emotional mirroring]. And honestly, that’s just one of many issues we consistently help practices overcome, like [name a few].”

Question: “Do any of these additional problems resonate with you? That way I can gear the demo to how we can help.”
This is the single most important slide for tailoring the call. Use their literal words from slide 4. The demo you run next should be aimed at whatever they confirm here.
Brands
16 The solution the biggest brands use
“We solve these through the same solution the biggest brands in the world use to grow revenue. Can you guess what it is? Every major brand has their own mobile rewards app — and that’s not a coincidence. They have the dollars to invest in any solution, and they all use this as a core one. And all of them see higher sales and more profitability every year.”
Mobile App
17 Rewards, AI, and 24/7 shopping in your own app
“That’s why we built a platform that combines the power of rewards, AI, and 24/7 shopping inside your own branded mobile app. It creates a completely new source of revenue while giving your patients the best experience in the industry.”
Social proof
18 Patients love it — Top 200 app, 16,000+ reviews
“The response from patients has been incredible. Since launch, RepeatMD has ranked in the Top 200 apps worldwide, consistently outperforming platforms like eBay, Olo, and Puma in engagement. With 16,000-plus five-star reviews, this isn’t just another tech tool — it’s something patients genuinely love and use.”

Soft commitment check: “Obviously you haven’t seen the full platform yet, but just hearing how other practices use it to drive revenue and retention — would it be fair to say this feels like something worth a closer look?”
The transition into the demo
QR onboarding
19 How a patient gets onto the app
“Let me show you what it looks like for your patient to get onto your app. First they scan a QR code we provide. They get a reward just for signing up, which is the key to getting them on. Sign-up takes under a minute, then they’re discovering everything you offer in a 24/7 e-commerce experience.”

This is the handoff into the live demo — continue in the Demo section.
Slides that surface during the demo
Skin Drop
23 No-inventory retail
“Skin Drop is one of our newest launches. In under ten minutes you choose your brands, select products, and start selling. These aren’t generic items — Obagi, SkinMedica, Plated, Revision, brands patients already trust. The best part: we handle inventory, fulfillment, shipping, even the upfront cost.”

Question: “Hard to imagine a simpler way to increase patient revenue, right?”
Sell while you sleep
26 The app makes money 24/7
“This is what I mean by selling while you sleep. With RepeatMD, your business stays open even when your doors are closed. Our clients consistently make the most sales between 9 and 11pm, relaxing at home, while their competition misses out — because you’re visible, shoppable, and your app is always working. Take a look at this screenshot a client sent us — she posted it on her Instagram, from her first week of launch.”
The process, the team, and the timeline
Old systems
29 Live in 30 days, three steps
“Old systems don’t deliver new results. If you’re still relying on outdated tools, it’s like trying to win today’s race with yesterday’s playbook. This is our proven three-step process to launch a new revenue engine in 30 days or less. Step one, we learn your specific goals. Step two, our team builds your custom-branded, white-labeled app. Step three, you go live — and that’s when the magic starts. You sell while you sleep, patients buy when your team is off the clock, and because it’s built around loyalty and automation, they keep coming back.”

Question: “Does launching in the next 30 or so days work for your timetable?”
The timeline question here is a soft timing check. If they hesitate, that’s your early read on a timing objection — carry it into the close and have the trailblazer reframe ready.
Onboarding
30 The team behind the tech
“What really separates RepeatMD, and why clients stay long-term, is the team behind the tech. From day one you’re not alone. You get a dedicated Onboarding Specialist who learns your goals and leads the setup with our Branding and Professional Services teams, so you don’t lift a finger. After you go live you’re supported by a Client Success Specialist, a service team for promotions and updates, 7-day support, and ongoing live masterclasses. When we say launch in 30 days, it’s because a full team is building it for you.”
The done-for-you team is the answer to the marketing-averse solo provider (“I know how to inject, I don’t know marketing”). Lean on it for that buyer.
Pricing
Pricing
32 The buildout and the three packages
“First, a one-time buildout of $6,997. That covers any apps you want to build now and in the future as you grow and add locations. Then three packages. Lite, $499 a month, is rewards and retention only — custom treatment plans for financing, unlimited text, automated retention — but no shop, so it can’t sell memberships, treatments, services, or packages. Professional adds the ability to sell memberships, but not treatments, services, or packages. To really grow, Executive is the only way — everything, plus selling treatments, services, and packages 24/7 on your app. To solve for [reiterate their problem], this is the plan for you.”

Present the standard numbers here. The first-call drop and the deeper lever live in the Pricing section — anchor high before you move them.
Value anchor
33 Trying to do it all yourself
“Our pricing is more than reasonable for any tier. If you tried to piece this together yourself — the various software, the automation, the support, the team — you’d easily be looking at $10,000 a month or more. So when I say our standard pricing is a steal, I mean it. But what I’m about to show you next makes it even easier to say yes.”
Remove the risk
Contracts
37 No long-term contracts
“For many practices the hesitation isn’t the idea, it’s the commitment. So let me be clear: no long-term contracts, no fine print, no gotchas. You’re never locked in. All we ask is your commitment to growth, because that’s what this is built for. If at any point it’s not the right fit, you can cancel with 90 days’ notice — simple, clean, and fair.”
This is the structural answer to the commitment objection, and the surgeon’s gate. For a hard no-contract policy, the written 90-day addendum (leadership named) is what converts it — see the Close and Objections sections.
Bonuses — the overdeliver close
Bonuses
38 Something for those ready to move
“If everything I’ve shown you was all you got, most people would already call that a win. But because we want to overdeliver right out of the gate, we’ve put together something special for those ready to move forward.”

Slides 39 to 42 are the bonus stack. Use them as reasons to act now; the corpus shows deals close on the core moves, so reach for these to sweeten a decision, not to carry one.
Bonus
39 Masterclass access
“Launching is just the beginning. You get weekly live masterclasses with some of the sharpest minds in the industry — Adam Haroun on brand strategy, Terri Ross and Dr. Amanda Holden on operations, Chris Balbi of Meesha Aesthetics, and Leisa Krauss on membership programs. These are $500-plus per seat and they’re included. While other practices guess their way through growth, you’re learning from the best.”
Bonus
40 Print package
“One of the biggest breakdowns in launching new tools is that no one promotes them in the office. So we include a complete print package, designed by our team, branded to your practice, shipped to your door — posters, QR code signs, counter cards. It’s a $1,500 value, and it means your team doesn’t have to guess how to roll this out.”
Bonus · urgency
41 VIP onboarding — the 48-hour lever
“We’re offering Premium VIP Onboarding as this month’s bonus, a $5,000 value. Your branded rewards menu, your shop, your custom app experience — all built by the time of your very first call. No waiting in line. This is normally reserved for larger clients, but if you send us your menu within 48 hours, it’s yours. Only if you can do that. This is our way of saying: if you’re ready, so are we.”
The 48-hour menu deadline is a genuine action-forcing mechanism — it asks for a small commitment that signals real intent. Use it to create momentum toward the close, not as pressure.
Bonus · ROI
42 Ecommerce marketing team
“Here’s where the ROI really kicks in. You get access to RepeatMD’s e-commerce marketing team — seasoned pros who run your yearly in-app promotions for you. Black Friday, holiday specials, membership offers, bundles, all strategized, designed, and launched by our team. It’s a true white-glove service valued at over $25,000 a year. Clients who participate see, on average, a 257% increase in transaction volume.”

Closing line: “Combine this with the VIP onboarding, and you’ve got the team, the platform, and the promotions — all done for you, ready to run.”
Five minutes of prep before the call is the difference between generic and specific. It’s also the clearest signal to the prospect that you did the work, and doing the work buys you permission to get specific in discovery. The strongest discovery calls in the corpus open with something the rep could only know by looking.
Minimum prep — five minutes
Research
Their website — two minutes
Scan the services menu, locations, named providers, and any visible tech (the EMR or booking tool, financing logos like Cherry or CareCredit, retail brands they carry). Look for one specific detail you can reference in the first three minutes. Whether they sell products online, whether they list memberships, and whether they offer wellness all change how you run the demo.
Research
Their socials — one minute
Instagram mostly. Posting cadence, before-and-afters, follower count, what they emphasize. You’re not researching to pitch them back at themselves, you’re researching to understand their voice before you talk to them. A practice that posts daily promos is a different conversation than one that posts twice a year.
Research
Existing or referred RepeatMD practice? Note the connection
If they’re a referral or a competitor of an existing customer, that’s leverage. Several of the strongest closes in the corpus involved a local-reference anchor: Maor came in pre-sold from a Miami referral and closed in thirty-six minutes; Nik’s competitor in town already runs RepeatMD, which turned into an underdog motivator rather than a deterrent. Know the connection before the call and you can name it.
Decision
Identify the archetype
Before the call starts, make a guess: surgeon-on-call, admin-as-buyer, solo wellness or DPC provider, returning prospect, co-decider, or multi-entity / enterprise. Your opener and your pace change based on which one you’re talking to. The six archetypes and how to read them are in the Discovery section.
Prepare
One specific opener tied to the research
Not flattery, not a rehearsed line. Something from your research that lets the prospect teach you something.
The shape that works:
• “I was on your site, you’ve got a pretty complete menu. I’m curious how you’re handling memberships today.”
• “I saw you’re carrying [retail brand], are you selling those online at all right now?”
• “You’ve been open about five years and you’re branching into aesthetics, walk me through that.”
• For a referral or competitor-of-a-customer: “I think you know [name] over at [practice], they’ve been running this a while.”
Discovery is not a list of questions to run through. It’s a conversation where you follow the prospect’s lead and drill into what they volunteer. The deck’s opener (“what are some of the biggest challenges you’ve found in growing your practice today?”) gets you the first answer; the drills below get you to the real one. Six archetype tracks set the opening, a handful of universal drills apply on every call.
The archetype map — your opener branches here
Track A
Surgeon on the call
Signal: The surgeon-owner is physically on the call, not an administrator. Plastics and derm. They interrogate the product directly and decide in the room.

Opener: Respect the time, lead with the economic lever, not the feature tour. Surgeons run real financing volume and audit their software stack.

“You’re running six figures a year through financing — let me start there, because that’s where the math is most obvious.”

Why it works: Surgeons are skeptical and fast. Kristopher ran a live competitor Google search mid-call and named a no-long-term-contract policy from a prior vendor burn; Azar raised Stark and anti-kickback compliance head-on and audited ten-plus subscriptions for overlap; Bill named the ASAPS peer-check as his gate. The objections are sharper than any other archetype, but the close is faster when handled right.
The move: don’t fight the skepticism, structure around it. The written 90-day addendum (leadership named, overriding the standard contract language) is the answer to the no-contract objection — it took Kristopher about two minutes from objection to card-on-call once it was structured around his policy. Respect peer-validation gates; surgeons trust other surgeons.
Track B
Admin or manager buying on behalf of the surgeon
Signal: A practice administrator, PCC, or marketing director is on the call and the actual decision-maker isn’t. Often new to the role or the vertical.

Opener: Acknowledge the structure early and make them the internal champion.

“You’ll be the one walking this back to [the owner], so I want to make sure you leave with what you need to make that case, not just a demo.”

Why it works: The buyer becomes your advocate in a room you’re not in. Kristin onboarded for her surgeon, Cindy for hers, Tiffany evaluated for Dr. McGregor, Dana the marketing director, Ivette across three locations, Sandra explicitly pitching RepeatMD over billboards and radio to her CEO.
The move: ship the recording proactively, it carries the internal sell. Arm the champion with the comparison math. Don’t push for card-on-call — respect the chain. Follow up after the decision-maker has been looped in.
Track C
Solo wellness or DPC provider
Signal: Single-operator NP, DPC, or wellness practice, often self-financing, sometimes having just bought a device. The owner is the provider, the front desk, and the marketer.

Opener: Qualify the budget reality early and lead with structure, not features.

“Before I show you anything — is this the kind of investment that works for where the practice is right now, or are we planning around something later this year?”

Why it works: This archetype judges the monthly against an already-stacked software bill or a hard personal cash constraint. Asking early prevents the long discount ladder that doesn’t close a structural affordability wall. Jennifer/Superior declined on affordability after a long ladder that a single early question would have surfaced; the lesson is qualify first.
The move: for the provider whose real problem is their own pricing discipline (Sheena: “set pricing will stop me over-giving”), the platform sells as a structural guardrail, not a feature set. Lead with member-vs-non-member pricing as the mechanism that takes the discounting decision out of their hands. Name the honest Light-vs-Executive trade-off rather than pretending Executive hits a number it can’t.
Track D
Returning prospect
Signal: They demoed before, months or years ago, and came back. Often reference-warmed or returning because the product evolved or their timing changed.

Opener: Let them tell the origin story. Ask how they got back here.

“I see we talked a while back — what brought you back around to looking at this now?”

Why it works: The origin story is the first trust-building moment and tells you what changed. April returned after seeing the product evolve and closed; Sultana came back reference-warmed two years later; Ryan came back via peer recommendation. Compress discovery, don’t waste the warmth on generic questions.
The move: shorten the call. A returning prospect doesn’t need convincing, they need the gap closed and the path to live. Confirm what changed, answer the specific questions, set up the close.
Track E
Co-decider call — two or more on the line
Signal: Owner plus office manager, two partners, or owner plus an absent stakeholder’s proxy. Daniel + Revital, Don + Hannah, Teresa + Samantha, Nik + Donna, Nicole + Charlie.

Opener: Pace for the room, not the lead voice. Acknowledge both people and find out who decides what.

“Before we dive in — how do you two split this? Who’s thinking about the day-to-day and who’s thinking about the dollars?”
The move: co-decider calls run longer, get more questions per minute, and close slower but with higher commit when they land. The rep who paces for the room does better. Don’t let the quieter decider go unaddressed — the office manager who handles the phones (Donna) often surfaces the real operational objection.
Track F
Multi-entity / enterprise
Signal: Multiple locations, separated bank accounts, an MSO or PLC structure, or a plastics-plus-medspa group. Tiffany / Peninsula, Kristin / Vega, Mike Reno / BWell (four locations, PLC + MSO).

Opener: Don’t pretend the entity question away. Surface the structure early because it shapes billing routing, gift-card mechanics, and revenue allocation.

“Walk me through the entity structure — how many locations, and do they share a bank account or are they separated?”
The move: the cleanest fallback is one app branded to the consumer-recognized entity, all payouts to one bank account, with scheduled per-line-item revenue reports back to the practice. Separate banks require separate apps (the Ivette canonical case). These calls reward sophistication and enterprise/per-location pricing.
The drill moves — use on every call regardless of archetype
Drill 1
“Who’s doing that today?”
When the prospect describes a process (running promos, following up with leads, managing the membership), ask who specifically does it. Owner? Front desk? A remote office manager? Whoever’s free?

Why it matters: you need to know whose job changes when RepeatMD goes live. Owner-doing-everything (Nik, before he signed) signals a different sell than a practice with a dedicated coordinator. The AI and self-service app is most valuable where the owner is the bottleneck.
Drill 2
“How does that work, mechanically?”
When they name a revenue or retention motion (“we sell memberships,” “we do weight loss,” “we carry retail”), ask for the specifics.

• Memberships — how many active, what do they pay, how do they sign up?
• Weight loss — per-dose or packaged? Are you losing any to the online compounders?
• Retail — in-practice only, or online? Which brands move?

Why it matters: “we have a membership” is the answer. “I built one in my EMR and I’ve sold one” (Maria) is the real story. The mechanics are where the pain lives, and the existing-but-broken membership is the single most common finding in the corpus.
Drill 3
“What happened when you tried that?”
If they’ve tried something (an agency, another platform, a financing tool, a membership), ask what happened.

• You looked at [competitor] — what made you decide against it?
• You set up Cherry — how’s the approval rate been? (Several practices were declined by Cherry directly — that’s the financing-via-umbrella opening.)
• You built a membership already — why isn’t it selling?

Why it matters: pain history is the cleanest path to the value prop. A practice that was declined by Cherry (Teresa, Nik) hands you the umbrella unlock for free.
Drill 4
“What’s next on your roadmap over the next 90 days?” — most overlooked question
Ask this on every call. Every call.

Why it matters: it catches capital and timing conflicts before you’ve invested twenty-five minutes pitching. A practice opening a new location, mid-EMR-migration, or that just spent $80K on a device has a timing shape that changes the close. Jennifer/Superior had just bought an Alma Opus — asked at minute three, the affordability constraint structures the whole call instead of detonating it at the end. A practice with a slow summer ahead is a trailblazer-reframe setup (Nik).
Drill 5
Follow the thread
When the prospect volunteers something specific, drill before moving on. Don’t collect and file, collect and ask the next question.
