• “We’re losing weight-loss patients to the online options” → “How many, roughly? What are they leaving for?”
• “I hate holding inventory” → “So how are you handling retail today — and what would no-inventory retail be worth?” (the Skin Drop opening)
• “I’m too giving on pricing” → “Tell me what that looks like — where do you cut deals you shouldn’t?”

This is where the best calls get their gold. The quality of the call is proportional to how specifically you listen.
Bridge to the demo when their story is done, not when your list is done
Cue to bridge: they’ve told you about their revenue motions, their team, and their roadmap, volunteered a recent pain point, and asked something product-specific like “so how does this actually work?”

Bridge: “OK, that’s really helpful. Let me show you what this looks like, and we’ll talk specifically about how it fixes [the thing they named].”

The bridge references something they told you. It proves discovery wasn’t a waste of their time, and it sets up a demo aimed at their lever instead of a generic feature tour.
The eighteen-step patient app walkthrough is the proven piece — every rep who runs it cleanly gets engagement. What separates good demos from bad ones is the thirty seconds before you share the screen, configuring the app to match the prospect, and referencing what they told you in discovery as you move through it. Walk the app the way a patient would: Home, Rewards, Discover, Shop, financing, the AI advisor, Skin Drop, then flip to Admin.
Before you share the screen — thirty-second config check
Critical
Think before you click
• Do they offer wellness or weight loss? If so, the GLP-1 and membership story is central. If not, don’t confuse them with services they don’t have.
• Do they sell retail today, online or in-practice? That sets up the Skin Drop moment.
• Did they name a specific lever in discovery (memberships, financing, after-hours sales, retail)? Lead the Shop walkthrough with that one.
• Are they inventory-averse? Note it — Skin Drop lands hardest there.
• Are they price-sensitive or affordability-constrained? Plan to anchor the membership math against their own service pricing.
Thirty seconds of thought. It’s the difference between a demo that references their practice and one that feels like a canned tour.
The opening — how a patient gets on the app
1QR onboarding. “Let me show you what it looks like for your patient to get onto your app. They scan a QR code we provide, and they get a reward just for signing up — that’s the hook that gets them on. Sign-up takes under a minute, then they’re into a 24/7 experience.” The signup reward is the adoption mechanic; name it.
2Home screen and Rewards. Walk the home screen, then Rewards. Point out that the app surfaces the AI advisor (Adonis) and personalized recommendations right on home — flag it now and say you’ll come back to it. Then return to finish the home tour.
Shop — the revenue engine, led by their lever
3Discover, then Shop. Use the top scroll to anchor on what they told you: “You mentioned you wanted to sell more [X] — not only do we help with that, you’ll see we help you sell anything your practice offers.” Left to right: promotions, memberships, treatments, products, packages, gift cards.
4Gift cards and products. Ask as you go: “Are you selling gift cards today — online or in store?” and “I see you are / aren’t selling products on your site today.” Tease that you have a way to accelerate product sales and add brands with a better patient experience — you’ll come back to it (the Skin Drop setup).
5Memberships and treatments. Walk both. This is where the existing-but-broken membership pain gets answered live: show member-vs-non-member pricing on every transaction, hands-off billing, and the membership selling itself in an app instead of sitting unused in an EMR. For the over-giving solo provider, this is the structural-discipline moment — the pricing decision is taken out of their hands.
6Packages and financing. On packages, bring it to checkout and show the multiple ways to buy, including best-in-class financing. “This isn’t just a better patient experience — it’s more competitive than how you’re doing it today.”
Live moment
7 The Affirm financing reveal
Show the Affirm rate and emphasize 24/7 financing. This is the single highest-leverage tactical moment when the prospect already names a financing tool: the flat 3.99% + $0.30 comparison against Cherry/CareCredit’s variable, term-dependent fees. For practices Cherry declined directly (Teresa, Nik), this is also the access unlock — under the RMD umbrella they get approved at all, with the higher per-transaction limit. Deliver it specifically, not as a script.
It keeps working when you’re closed
8Sell while you sleep. “Not only does the app sell 24/7, it keeps working for you 24/7.” The deck’s framing: clients consistently make the most sales between 9 and 11pm, when their doors are closed and the competition is missing out. This lands — it’s a concrete, memorable image of after-hours revenue.
The AI advisor — Adonis
9Adonis, the AI treatment advisor. Come back to what you flagged on the home screen. Walk through what Adonis is, then use it against a challenge they raised in discovery: make a live recommendation, show the recommended treatments, and show how a patient can buy right there or book. “The app makes recommendations when patients ask — and it keeps recommending in the ‘just for you’ and ‘chosen for you’ sections.”
10Tie it back to products. “You mentioned wanting to sell more products — this is how, with custom recommendations. The best part: these work for the products you have in practice today, or you can use our drop-shipping solution, Skin Drop.”
Skin Drop — the inventory-free retail close
Lands hardest with inventory-averse buyers
11 Skin Drop
“In under ten minutes you choose your brands, select products, and start selling. These aren’t generic items — Obagi, SkinMedica, Plated, Revision, brands patients already trust. We handle inventory, fulfillment, shipping, even the upfront cost.” In the corpus this is the cleanest match between pain and feature: providers who say “I hate holding inventory” (Sheena, Maria) light up here. The margin clarification is worth having ready — drop-shipped retail runs around 30% margin shipped, versus owned in-store-pickup product at full margin (Nik’s Korean skincare). Skin Drop is also the practical reason to be on the Executive tier.
12Flip to Admin. Once the patient-side story lands, move to the owner-facing view — covered in the next section.
Talk-ratio check
If you’ve been talking for five-plus minutes without a question or a reaction, stop and ask one. Even mid-demo.

• “What’s your reaction to this so far?”
• “Does this match what you’d want your patient experience to feel like?”
• “Where does this land for you?”

Then stop talking and wait for the answer. The answer tells you whether the demo is landing or whether you’re demoing the wrong lever.
The owner-facing side answers two questions every serious buyer asks: who runs this day to day, and does it replace what I’m already paying for? The dashboard, reporting, promotions engine, two-way messaging, and the EMR/booking integration pause. The done-for-you positioning matters most here, because the recurring fear is “another tool I have to manage.”
The dashboard and reporting
Walk the owner view: revenue by source, transaction volume, membership counts, what’s selling. The contrast that lands is visibility — most practices can’t see after-hours or app-driven revenue separately today. For multi-location and multi-entity practices, this is where you show scheduled per-line-item revenue reports back to each location.
Promotions and the done-for-you team
The retention answer
You don’t build this alone
The deck’s onboarding story is real leverage here: a dedicated Onboarding Specialist, Branding and Professional Services teams that build the app so the owner doesn’t lift a finger, then a Client Success Specialist and service team for promotions after go-live. For the marketing-averse solo provider (“I know how to inject, I don’t know marketing” — Sheena), package-building-done-for-you is a primary value driver, not a side feature. Submit the menu, the team builds the recommended packages, a strategy call approves them before launch.
Two-way messaging
Walk the messaging modes: full automation, manual, or hybrid. This is the answer to the owner-is-the-bottleneck problem — the AI handles the routine follow-up so the owner gets their time back. For practices where the owner does everything (Nik, before signing), this is the time-savings story that justifies the whole platform.
EMR / booking integration pause
Good instinct
Pause here and ask about their stack
If it hasn’t come up: “Remind me — what are you using for your EMR and booking today?” Be specific about what RepeatMD does and doesn’t replace: it’s the patient-facing experience, rewards, financing, and membership management — not a replacement for booking or EMR. When a prospect is shopping an EMR transition, recommending Aesthetic Record (the EMR-recommendation-as-discovery-value-add move) builds trust at no rep-discretion cost. When sprawl is named (the practice juggling five tools that almost talk to each other), run the cost-of-current-stack diagnostic before defending price.
The data per lead
Show the full contact record and how much more the practice captures per patient than they do today. “Right now, what do you capture on a lead?” They’ll name name, email, phone. You just showed them far more — intent, treatment interest, purchase history.
Anchor high, present the first-call offer, hold one deeper lever in reserve. Three packages, all built on a one-time platform buildout. The discipline that matters: present the standard price first so the first-call offer reads as the concession it is, and don’t burn the deeper lever against a structural affordability wall a discount can’t close.
The three packages
Lite — $499/mo. Custom treatment plans for financing, unlimited text messaging, automated retention. Rewards and retention only — no shop, so it can’t sell memberships, treatments, services, or packages.

Professional — $699/mo. Everything in Lite, plus the ability to sell memberships. Still can’t sell treatments, services, or packages.

Executive — $999/mo standard. Everything, plus selling treatments, services, and packages 24/7, and Skin Drop. For any practice whose lever is med-commerce, retail, or full membership monetization, this is the only tier that fits.
The buildout and the anchor
One-time platform buildout: $6,997. Covers every app they want to build now and in the future as they add locations. Present this as the standard before any discount.

The justification, straight from the deck: “If you tried to piece this together yourself — the software, the automation, the support, the team — you’d easily be looking at $10,000 a month or more.” Establish the value at standard pricing first. The drop only feels like a concession if they heard the anchor.
The first-call offer
Standard close offer
$1,049 build · $799/mo Executive
The first-call promotional pricing: buildout drops from $6,997 to $1,049, Executive from $999 to $799/mo. Month-to-month, no long-term contract, 90-day cancellation. This is the offer most deals close on. Present it as a real, time-bound first-call concession, not a number you’ll obviously beat.
The deeper lever — in reserve
Use as an accelerator, not a ladder
$500 build · $699/mo · 50% off the first month
The deeper lever a rep can reach for: buildout at $500, Executive at $699/mo, with 50% off the first month.

When to use it: as a close accelerator at the moment of decision — the way Nellie applied $500 off the build the instant Nik moved to sign. Not as a rung in a ladder you climb against resistance.
The anti-pattern to avoid: the long discount ladder against a buyer whose constraint is structural. Jennifer/Superior got a drawn-out ladder (manager twice unreachable) and still declined, because the wall was real affordability, not price perception. Qualify affordability early (Track C). If the number genuinely doesn’t work, the honest move is the Light tier or a deliberate revisit after their next revenue event — not a deeper cut that trains the buyer to keep pushing.
No long-term contract
Straight from the deck and confirmed across the corpus: no long-term contracts, month-to-month, cancel with 90 days’ notice (three-month minimum). This is the structural answer to the commitment objection, and it’s also the surgeon’s gate — the written 90-day addendum (leadership named) is what converted Kristopher’s no-contract policy into a card-on-call.
Bonuses — available levers
The deck carries first-call bonuses worth naming when they help: weekly live masterclasses with industry experts ($500+/seat value), a branded print package for in-office rollout ($1,500 value), VIP onboarding with the app pre-built if they send their menu within 48 hours ($5,000 value), and the ecommerce marketing team running their yearly in-app promotions ($25,000/yr value). Use these as reasons-to-act-now and overdeliver signals. In practice, the corpus shows deals close on the core moves — card-on-call, the trailblazer reframe, financing-via-umbrella — so reach for bonuses to sweeten a decision, not to carry one.
Card on the call is the gold standard — tomorrow is a deal you can lose, now is a deal you’ve closed. The trailblazer reframe is the timing-objection answer and the closest thing the corpus has to a signature close lever. DocuSign in session converts a verbal yes into a signed deal. Reciprocal follow-up is the safety net when they genuinely can’t close live.
Card on the call — the gold standard
Best outcome
Take the card live, send the DocuSign in session
When the prospect is warm and decided: “Great — let’s get you set up. I’ll take the card now and send the agreement to sign while we’re on, takes about a minute.” Take the card live (enter it yourself, don’t write it down), send the DocuSign in session, capture the legal entity and CC emails, and trigger onboarding before you hang up. Maor, Maria, and Nik all closed this way. Sending a link to sign later is how a verbal yes evaporates.
The trailblazer reframe — the timing-objection answer
Signature lever
Reframe a Q3 delay against the cost of going live cold
Trigger: “Let’s revisit in a few months,” “not the best time,” “I’ve got a lot going on this summer.”

The move: reframe the delay against the revenue calendar. “If there’s any way to start in May or June — the summer’s a little slower, which means you have time to actually learn the tool and get fluent. Start in August and you’re going live cold right into your busiest quarter: Halloween, Black Friday, the holidays. Your trailblazers are the clients who get going now and are running by the time the money months hit.”

Then stop talking. This is exactly how Nellie converted Nik from an August revisit to signing on the call. Pair it with the time-savings logic — the tool gives back the very hours the delay was meant to protect.
Financing access via the umbrella — the unlock close
When a practice was declined by Cherry directly (Teresa, Nik), the umbrella is a capability they can’t otherwise get, not just a better rate: under RepeatMD’s 4,000-practice umbrella they get Affirm approval at a flat 3.99%, higher approval rates, and a higher per-transaction limit. For a practice expanding into higher-ticket aesthetics, that unlock can be the deciding factor.
Reciprocal follow-up — the backup
If they genuinely can’t close live: “Sounds like you want to talk to [partner / team]. Here’s what I’ll do — I’ll send the recording and a recap. If I haven’t heard from you by [day], I’ll reach out [day after]. Sound good?” Make it mutual so it’s a commitment, not a brush-off. For the admin-as-buyer archetype, the recording does the internal selling in the room you’re not in.
Talk-ratio reality check
Prospects who talk 30 to 50% of the time in the first ten minutes close more often than ones who talk under 20%. If you catch yourself talking five-plus minutes without a question or response, stop and ask one. Then sit with the silence — the most-missed close skill is letting the question land instead of answering it yourself.
The objections that come up on nearly every call, each with the answer the corpus shows actually working. These are the compressed, in-the-moment versions for use mid-call. The fuller treatment — surface form, underlying concern, response motion, traps to avoid, and source attribution for each — lives in the Objections tab, which is the canonical reference. Use these to recognize and respond in the moment; read the Objections tab to go deep.
1. Timing / “we just bought a device”
Most common
Reframe the delay, don’t accept it
Who raises it: practices that recently made a big commitment (a new machine, a new location, a platform contract) or are heading into a busy stretch. They frame it as timing, not price.

The answer: the trailblazer reframe (see Close). A Q3 start means going live cold into the busy quarter; a Q2 start uses the slow summer to get fluent. The bridge offer also comes in two flavors — one-month-free plus reduced setup, or the deeper lever — matched to how much breathing room they actually need.
2. Affordability — solo and DPC providers
Common in wellness
Qualify early, then right-size honestly
Who raises it: single-operator wellness, DPC, and NP practices judging the monthly against a stacked software bill or a hard cash constraint, sometimes right after buying a device.

The answer: qualify the budget reality early (Track C) so you’re not discovering the wall at the close. If the number genuinely doesn’t work, the honest move is the Light tier or a deliberate revisit after their next revenue event — not a long discount ladder. Naming the real Light-vs-Executive trade-off beats pretending Executive hits a number it can’t. Jennifer/Superior is the cautionary case: a long ladder against a structural wall still ended in a decline.
3. “I already have a membership / I’ve tried that”
Most frequent finding
Fix what’s broken, don’t introduce memberships
Who raises it: the majority of the corpus. They built a membership in their EMR or Shopify and it sits unused, or they sold one and gave up.

The answer: reframe from “introduce memberships” to “fix what’s broken.” The membership doesn’t sell itself in an EMR; it sells itself in an app where the patient sees member-vs-non-member pricing on every page, with hands-off billing. For the over-giving provider, the app is the discipline that stops the ad-hoc discounting. Reps who reframe this early go faster.
4. Financing — “I already use Cherry / CareCredit”
Common
Make it the live competitive moment
The answer: the head-to-head comparison is the highest-leverage tactical move when they name a tool. Affirm at a flat 3.99% + $0.30 regardless of term, against Cherry/CareCredit’s variable, sliding-scale fees. Frame it as “flat-rate practice fee versus variable-rate sliding scale,” not “ours is better.” And if they were declined by Cherry, pivot to the umbrella access unlock — they get approved at all under RepeatMD. Deliver it specifically; reps who recite it generically don’t get the acknowledgment.
5. “I already have too many tools / another vendor”
Common
Run the cost-of-current-stack diagnostic
Who raises it: operationally rigorous practices juggling five tools that almost talk to each other and wary of a sixth.

The answer: be specific about what RepeatMD does and doesn’t replace — patient-facing experience, rewards, financing, membership management; not booking or EMR. When sprawl is named, total up the line items it consolidates before defending the price. The wedge is “here’s how this replaces three or four things you’re already paying for,” not “here’s another great app.”
6. “I don’t want to be locked into a contract”
Common, esp. surgeons
Acknowledge, then point to the structure
Who raises it: anyone burned by a prior vendor’s long contract, surgeons especially.

The answer: no long-term contract, month-to-month, 90-day cancellation. For a hard no-contract policy (Kristopher), the written 90-day addendum with leadership named, overriding the standard agreement language, is what converts it — it took about two minutes from objection to card-on-call. Don’t defend, don’t deflect; point to the structural answer.
7. “I saw some negative reviews”
Occasional
Acknowledge, own, pivot to the fix
The answer: acknowledge specifically, own it without defensiveness, and pivot to what changed structurally (the no-contract terms, the support model). Don’t argue the reviews. The pattern that works is first-person ownership plus the concrete fix, then back to their goals.
Go deeper — the full Objections tab
The seven above are the in-the-moment versions. The Objections tab is the canonical reference and goes well beyond them: eleven recurring objection shapes plus the sales-pace shapes, each broken down into surface form, underlying concern, the response motion the strongest calls used, the traps to avoid, and the specific calls where it was observed. It also covers shapes not summarized here — the EMR-sync question that surfaces on nearly every call, convenience-fee confusion, and the co-decider and timing dynamics. Read it once to build the deep version; use this section to recognize and respond live.

The War Room

Thirty-two calls from five reps, surfaced and analyzed for the patterns that repeat. Each call is a real Gong recording with what worked, what didn't, and what the team can learn. Switch between the patterns view and the call-by-call view using the toggle below.

Nothing matches
Try a different search term or clear the filter.

Eleven patterns surfaced across the thirty-two-call corpus. Eight cleared the three-instance threshold and have cross-rep evidence; they're shown as formal patterns. The wellness expansion (eight new calls) promoted GLP-1 patient leakage from watching to a firmly formal pattern at seven instances across three reps, and surfaced a new solo-provider affordability pattern. Three remain at two instances and are tracked as “watching” until a third instance confirms or refutes them.

Formal patterns · 3+ instances, cross-rep
10 instances · Cross-rep

Existing-but-broken membership

The majority of prospects already have a membership program, but it isn’t producing. Pricing is wrong, staff don’t push it, or the structure doesn’t match how the practice actually sells. The pitch is “fix what’s broken,” not “introduce memberships.” Reps who reframe early go faster. The wellness expansion strengthened this further: a distinct solo-provider variant shows up where the membership works but the selling and tracking are entirely manual and bottlenecked on the owner, and a hormone-practice variant where an annual treatment plan functions as a membership with no cross-sell hook attached.

Where it shows up: Marie (since 2008, ineffective), Cindy ($99 banking, “just there”), Kristin (“ones that go every month but no incentive”), Marcela (“they’re just there”), Mike BWell (tried, failed at price point), Tabia (haven’t built one), Tiffany (none yet), Don/Renew (annual hormone plan, no cross-sell hook), Jennifer/Superior (age-based tiers underprice functional-medicine time), Nicole/Blue River (one $150 membership, fully manual).
6 instances · Cross-rep

Patient financing comparison is the live competitive moment

When the prospect names existing financing (Cherry, CareCredit, PatientFi, Allē Pay), the 3.99% flat comparison is the highest-leverage tactical move in the demo. Reps who deliver it specifically (rate, structure, fee transparency) get acknowledgment. Reps who deliver it as a script don’t. The wellness cohort surfaced a distinct sub-pattern: financing access via the RepeatMD umbrella. Practices that Cherry or Affirm declined directly get approved under RepeatMD’s 4,000-practice umbrella, which converts the conversation from a rate comparison into a capability the practice cannot otherwise obtain.

Where it shows up: Bill (Cherry + CareCredit, asked rate), Kristin (CareCredit + PatientFi capped spending), Shannon (Crowley-referenced setups), Vicky (Cherry-for-Allē-vs-everything-else breakdown by Taylor). Umbrella-access variant: Teresa/Integrity (declined by Cherry, approved via umbrella), Nik/Primary Choice (declined by Cherry, approved via umbrella — closed).
8 instances · Cross-rep

Co-decider calls behave differently

Calls with two or more decision-makers on the line run longer, get more questions per minute, surface more granular product concerns, and close slower but with higher commit when they land. Different cadence required. The rep who paces for the room rather than the lead voice does better.

Where it shows up: Daniel + Revital (Bliss Wellness, mobile concierge), Marcela + Chris (Skin Avenue), Shannon + Jennifer (Younger Aesthetics, owner + esthetician), Mike Reno + Kent + Berg + Shane (BWell, 4 stakeholders), Don + Hannah (Renew, owner + practice manager), Teresa + Samantha (Integrity, office manager + owner), Nik + Donna (Primary Choice, owner + office manager — closed), Nicole + Charlie (Blue River, owner + pain-management doctor listening).
3 instances · Cross-rep

Multi-entity / MSO structures in sophisticated buyers

Plastics-plus-medspa practices and physician-owned groups are typically structured with separated bank accounts, separate entities, or MSO arrangements. Each surfaces real product-fit questions about billing routing, gift-card mechanics, and revenue allocation. These calls reward sophistication and need handling that doesn’t pretend the entity question away.

Where it shows up: Tiffany / Peninsula (PS + medspa + ASC, separated banks), Kristin / Vega (PS + medspa, separated banks), Mike Reno / BWell (Lakeway Medical Clinic PLC + BWell Holdings MSO, 4 locations).
3 instances · Cross-rep

Convenience fee creates confusion on first explanation

The 2.5% patient convenience fee plus the 2.79%/3.99% practice fee structure is genuinely hard to land on first pass. Prospects re-ask, ask who pays which, and need a third pass to confirm. The current explanation requires 3+ exchanges to land. A tighter explainer would save time and reduce post-call confusion.

Where it shows up: Marie (“wait back up”), Kristin (asked specifically who gets it), Daniel (re-confirmed “the client pays, not us”).
5 instances · Cross-rep

Time-pressure budget objection

Prospects who recently made a large commitment (a new machine, a new location, an existing platform contract) raise budget objections framed as timing rather than pricing. The bridge offer comes in two flavors: one-month-free plus reduced setup, or multi-month half-off. The right flavor matches how much breathing room the prospect needs. The strongest handle in the corpus is the trailblazer reframe: reframe a Q3 delay against the cost of going live cold into the busy quarter, versus using the slow summer to get fluent before the money months.

Where it shows up: Jennifer (just bought $80K TruSculpt, one-month-free + reduced setup), Marie (just opened second location, 4-month discount on the new location only), Cindy (paying 9% to Moxie through August, 3-month bridge), Jennifer/Superior (just bought Alma Opus, timing-plus-affordability — declined), Nik/Primary Choice (wanted to delay to August, converted now via the trailblazer reframe — closed).
7 instances · Cross-rep

GLP-1 patient leakage to online compounders and telehealth

Weight-loss patients are besieged by online prescribers, compounding pharmacies, and telehealth brands (Hims, Hers, NewSelf) offering semaglutide and tirzepatide at $79-100/month. Once a patient switches, the practice loses them entirely. This was a near-pattern at two instances when the wellness vertical was thin; the wellness expansion surfaced five more clear instances across three reps, formalizing it. The retention answer is consistent: the auto-deducted weight-loss membership with bundled services (coaching, check-ins, NAD/IV add-ons) that the price-only online option structurally cannot match — convert the one-off price-sensitive buyer into a VIP subscriber so the ad stops landing. The wellness-bank membership is the structural cousin for practices where dollars matter more than dose-volume.

Where it shows up: Mike BWell (compounders at $79 vs $199 in-clinic), Daniel/Bliss ($192-tirzepatide online vendors), Jennifer/Superior (Hims/Hers at $100/mo), Sultana/Be The Change (online ad pressure on hormone/weight-loss patients), Nicole/Blue River (insurance-to-cash switch, online options), Nik/Primary Choice (weight loss is least-generating, losing to NewSelf at $100 — closed), Sheena/Sheena Rose (weight-loss volume dropping to online options). Three reps: Nellie, Taylor, Remy.
3 instances · Cross-rep

Solo-provider affordability and price-resistance

Solo wellness and DPC providers — often single-operator, self-financing, sometimes having just bought a device — judge the monthly price against an already-stacked software bill or a hard personal cash constraint, not against the value in isolation. The coaching lesson is in the timing: when a buyer flags the constraint early, qualify the budget reality up front and either right-size to the Light plan or set a deliberate post-revenue-event revisit, rather than running a long discount ladder against a structural constraint a discount can’t close. Naming the honest Light-vs-Executive trade-off beats pretending the Executive can hit the buyer’s number.

Where it shows up: Jennifer/Superior (single-mom solo DPC, just bought Opus, declined on affordability after a long discount ladder), Sultana/Be The Change (wants $299-399 vs $699-799, judged against stacked software costs), Sheena/Sheena Rose (price nerves, deliberate frugal temperament). Two reps: Taylor, Nellie. Coaching: qualify affordability early; don’t burn discount ladders against structural constraints.
Watching · 2 instances, not yet formal

Objections are information. They tell you what the buyer is actually worried about.

The objections below are the ones that recur across the twenty-four calls in the war room. Each card has the surface form (how it shows up), the underlying concern (what the buyer is actually asking), the response motion (how the strongest calls handled it), the traps to avoid, and source attribution to the calls where it was observed. Pattern thresholds: one instance is a surfacing, two is a near-pattern, three is formal. Cards below are sorted by section, not by frequency — an objection that hit once but was structurally distinctive can matter as much as one that recurred five times.

Evidence base: Eleven recurring objection shapes across the twenty-four war room calls, plus two sales-pace shapes treated separately below. Frequencies are corpus counts, not generalizations. Where the corpus has fewer than three instances, the card is flagged as a near-pattern or singleton and the response motion is provisional.

Operational fit

five objection shapes · the prospect is testing whether the platform works in their actual workflow

These are the most common objections in the corpus and the ones reps will face on almost every call. The prospect has decided RepeatMD might be worth their attention; now they’re probing whether it fits the way they already run the practice. The corpus shows that operationally rigorous buyers are the most likely to surface these directly, and the most likely to close cleanly if they’re answered well.

“Does this sync with [our EMR]?”

4+ instances · load-bearing across nearly every Pass 4 call
Surface form Direct integration question, usually phrased as “does it sync with [NextTech / Vagaro / Zenoti / Boulevard / Aesthetic Record].” The prospect is mapping the platform onto their existing stack and wants to know if integration is full, partial, or a handoff. Surfaces in some form on nearly every call in Pass 4.
Underlying concern Whether the platform will create duplicate-entry work for the front desk, whether transactions will flow correctly, whether the prospect needs to switch EMRs to get full value. The concern isn’t whether integration exists in some abstract sense — it’s whether the practice’s actual EMR is the one that’s integrated.
Response motion: name the limitation plainly State it cleanly: “Aesthetic Record is the only EMR we have full two-way integration with today. For [their EMR], we link to your booking page so the patient can schedule from inside the app, but we don’t sync appointments or patient records back and forth.” Then describe what that actually means for the front desk — what they’ll see, what they won’t, what duplicate entry (if any) is required. When the prospect is already planning an EMR transition (Sara was considering it, Ryan was actively planning it), recommending Aesthetic Record becomes discovery value-add — competitive intelligence with no rep-discretion cost. Three corpus instances (Sara, Marcela, Ryan) elevate EMR-recommendation-as-discovery-value-add to a formal pattern.
Traps to avoid Don’t blur the EMR and the booking system as if they’re one handoff — they aren’t, and prospects who run both will hear the imprecision. Don’t say “it’s a handoff” without explaining what that means in workflow terms — vague phrasing reads as evasion even when the underlying answer is honest. Don’t claim broader integration than exists. Don’t say “we work with most EMRs” — sophisticated buyers will probe and the answer will come out anyway. The honest answer protects the trust line that the reviews-objection-defense playbook also depends on.

“We’re already running too many tools”

3 instances · formal pattern (Marcela, Tiffany, Azar)
Surface form Vendor sprawl articulated as a buying criterion, not a passing concern. Prospect describes a stack of three to ten-plus tools that aren’t talking to each other and names the operational consequence (double-bookings, manual reconciliation, missed leads) or the cumulative monthly cost. Now a formal pattern across two buyer shapes: operationally rigorous med spa admins managing multi-app stacks (Marcela, Tiffany) and fee-fatigued surgeons (Azar, who opened on 10-plus software packages at $300-500/month each and a stated need to take home dollars rather than spend them on more widgets).
“Everything’s done through Shopify and then Shopify apps… they don’t communicate with each other. Now we have an issue, double-booking.” Marcela (Skin Avenue, May 4, 2026)
Underlying concern Whether RepeatMD is the seventh tool that overlaps with the six they already have, or whether it actually replaces some of them. The prospect is doing cost-of-current-stack math in their head and wants the rep to do it with them rather than pretending the question isn’t there.
Response motion: run the cost-of-current-stack diagnostic When sprawl is named, run the diagnostic before the standard product walkthrough. Pitch wedge: “here’s how this replaces three or four line items” rather than “here’s another great app.” Ask which tools they’d drop first if they could collapse the stack. Anchor the response to specific line items the platform actually replaces (the manual membership management, the email-blast tool, the rewards-tracking spreadsheet, the gift card processor at 8%). Don’t over-claim — RepeatMD doesn’t replace EMR or booking, and saying it does will be checked.
Traps to avoid Don’t skip past the diagnostic into the demo. Don’t name vague replacements (“a bunch of your tools”). Don’t pretend the prospect’s existing tools aren’t working — some of them are, and the consolidation pitch is about which ones aren’t.

“Your AI doesn’t know my services / language”

2 instances · near-pattern (Sara plasma, Marcela Spanish)
Surface form Direct probe of the AI treatment advisor’s scope. Two corpus instances surfaced different shapes: Sara asked about Aria recognizing nitrogen plasma services (specialized regenerative aesthetics), Chris asked about Aria supporting Spanish-speaking patients for their McAllen client base. Both are real product limitations, not buyer hesitations dressed up as questions.
“On the AIs, just because being here in McAllen, Texas, we have a lot of clients that are Hispanic and they speak Spanish. Is it an only English AI?” Chris (Skin Avenue co-decider, May 4, 2026)
Underlying concern Whether the platform’s flagship feature will work for their specific patient population and service menu, or whether they’ll be paying for a feature that doesn’t apply. Plasma recognition is a service-menu coaching question (solvable in onboarding). Spanish-language support is a true product gap (not solvable in onboarding today).
Response motion: distinguish the two AI products, then answer honestly Clean distinction between the two AI products: Aria recommends services from the practice’s coached menu and can be trained on anything in the service list; Ageless is the clinical-imaging AI and has hard limits on what it recognizes. On the language gap, honest acknowledgment is the right move — faking it is worse than admitting the limitation. Alex’s execution on Marcela’s call (“we’re working on it… at the moment it’s just English”) is the right shape. Worth flagging to product team as a feature-request artifact for border-state and immigrant-heavy markets.
Traps to avoid Don’t conflate the two AI products (Aria vs Ageless). Don’t promise Spanish-language support is shipping soon if you don’t know the roadmap. Don’t pretend a service-menu gap is fixed if the onboarding consultant hasn’t been told.

“Now isn’t the right time”

4 instances · formal pattern (Jennifer time-pressure, Shannon July start, Marcela June move, Sara June launch)
Surface form Prospect names a specific operational event happening in the next four to eight weeks that they want to clear before adding the platform. Common shapes: a location move, a new device launch, seasonal busy period starting, a hire ramping up, an existing campaign mid-flight.
“I’m thinking if we do this, we’ll probably start it around July. That way we can get it built and train and get comfortable with it before September, like the fall where we get crazy.” Shannon (Younger Aesthetics, May 15, 2026)
Underlying concern Bandwidth, not interest. The prospect is genuinely thinking about implementation pacing rather than stalling. The structural problem is that a three-to-four week build-out from a commit today lines up almost exactly with the operational event they’re trying to clear — meaning the “wait until July” instinct usually doesn’t reflect when they’d actually want to be live.
Response motion: show the build-out math, thread the launch Three to four weeks from commit means the app lands two to three weeks before the operational event, leaving a pre-launch awareness window with existing patients. If the operational event is a launch (device, location, service line), thread the RepeatMD launch into the same event — one moment, one set of marketing, double the impact. If the prospect still wants buffer, offer the complimentary-second-month bridge as a structural hedge against launch slippage (Alex deployed this for Shannon).
Traps to avoid Don’t push card-on-call past a real timing constraint — the deal will close anyway and pushing past it creates implementation risk. Don’t schedule a callback without a calendar gate (Andy’s pending call lacks one and is the only Pass 4 call where that’s true). Don’t promise faster build-out than three to four weeks — the onboarding consultant will resent it.

“Is the referral reward compliant with anti-kickback / Stark?”

2 instances · near-pattern, plastics/derm-specific (Azar; Kristopher adjacent)
Surface form A surgeon or dermatologist asks directly whether the automated referral reward is legal under federal anti-kickback and Stark statutes, often having already turned down or constrained their own referral program for the same reason. This objection never appears in the med spa corpus — it is specific to physician-owned plastics and derm practices where regulatory exposure is taken seriously. Sometimes the prospect proposes a workaround themselves (a points system not tied to dollars), which is a trap the rep must not validate.
“Because of the Stark laws and the anti-kickback laws and all that, we’ve been very concerned about doing anything aggressive with regard to that.” Azar (Azar Plastic Surgery, Dec 30, 2025)
Underlying concern Whether adopting the platform creates legal liability for the practice. The surgeon is not asking for reassurance — they are asking for a precise answer about how the referral mechanic is configured, and they will notice if the rep waves it away or, worse, suggests a way to do something non-compliant quietly.
Response motion: confirm the compliant configuration, defer the rest to counsel The answer is narrow and firm. The referral reward can be configured receiver-only — an introductory offer to the new patient, with no kickback to the referrer — and that is the compliant configuration RepeatMD sets up by default. State plainly that this is how the practice will be configured. Then defer everything beyond it — automated sender kickbacks, manual back-end crediting, points workarounds — to the practice’s own legal counsel. This is the trust-building posture: a precise, bounded answer plus an honest handoff of what isn’t the rep’s to decide.
Traps to avoid Never characterize a non-compliant approach as acceptable because it is “harder to prove” or carries “no recourse” — this exact framing surfaced on the Azar call as a coaching flag and it exposes both the practice and RepeatMD to real liability. Don’t validate the prospect’s own proposed workaround just because they raised it. Don’t improvise a legal opinion — the rep is not the practice’s counsel, and saying so is the correct, trust-preserving move.

Trust & credibility

three objection shapes · the prospect is testing whether to give RepeatMD their attention at all

These objections show up before any operational or economic discussion lands. When they surface, they have to be addressed structurally — reps who try to skip past them into the standard pitch lose the deal regardless of how good the demo is. Less frequent than operational-fit objections, but more decisive when they hit.

“I was approached three years ago and didn’t buy”

3 instances · formal pattern (April, Ryan, Penny)
Surface form Prospect references a prior contact with RepeatMD — sometimes by name of the original rep or founder — and frames their current evaluation as a re-evaluation rather than a new consideration. Three returning-prospect calls in the corpus, each with a different recovery path: April returned after seeing product evolution, Ryan came back via peer recommendation from a current customer, Penny via timing change.
“I think about three years ago, I was approached by, I forget who it was anymore. When before Phil Sitter was a bazillionaire, and I didn’t know anything about it. I didn’t know how to use it. […] I said, I’m paying so much for my EMR system. And now I’m paying another one for this.” Dr. Ramos (Ageless Health & Aesthetics, Feb 5, 2026)
Underlying concern The original objection — usually additional-vendor cost or unclear product positioning — is still live in the prospect’s mind. They’re asking the rep to demonstrate what specifically has changed since the last contact. If the answer is “we’ve added features,” the answer is wrong — the prospect can read a changelog. They want to know whether the structural reason they passed before is still true.
Response motion: let the prior-contact history land before responding Don’t interrupt the recap. When they finish, name the product evolution in specifics (“the product has been changing and launching new versions even since October”) rather than in marketing speak. If the original objection was cost stacking on top of EMR, reframe as front-of-house-vs-back-of-house complement rather than another EMR replacement. Taylor’s execution on Ryan’s call is the cleanest in the corpus pre-audio-collapse.
Traps to avoid Don’t treat the prior contact as a hot lead. The prospect already passed once and is approaching this as a careful re-evaluation. Don’t push card-on-call on a returning prospect — they need the recording, the time to re-evaluate, and often a co-decider conversation. April closed dual-product on call, but only after the rep matched her pace.

“Y’all have terrible reviews”

1 instance · singleton, watching for second
Surface form Prospect raises RepeatMD’s public reputation directly. Usually comes early in the call, often before pricing has been discussed. Shannon raised it at 1:21 of her call after her warm-referral framing was already in place — she trusted Dr. Crowley but had read the reviews and wasn’t going to skip past them.
“I love Dr. Crowley and I trust him and I know he wouldn’t recommend something if he didn’t believe in it […] but like, y’all have terrible reviews.” Shannon (Younger Aesthetics, May 15, 2026)
Underlying concern Two specific complaint categories the prospect has already encountered: support deficits at early-stage scale, and long-term contract lock-in. The prospect isn’t asking RepeatMD to defend itself in the abstract — they’re asking the rep to demonstrate the company knows what went wrong and what changed.
Response motion: the four-step defense (Shannon-call playbook) Acknowledge directly without dismissing or over-apologizing. Identify the two specific complaint categories the prospect has read. Name the structural fixes (hundreds of new support hires, account executives now stay engaged post-sale, long-term contracts eliminated in favor of month-to-month with 90-day notice). Name the current top complaint honestly (integration depth with non-Aesthetic-Record EMRs, which is partner-side, not RepeatMD-side). Rep tenure framing (“I’ve been here since V1 V2 V3”) adds credibility without being defensive. Alex’s execution on this objection is the strongest moment in the corpus.
Traps to avoid Don’t dismiss the reviews. Don’t blame the customers. Don’t claim the reviews are fake. Don’t pivot to product features before the structural acknowledgment lands. If the prospect at 4:58 of the Shannon call says “that was what I read as well,” the diagnosis was correct — if they don’t, the rep is guessing at the wrong complaint category.

“I’m a doctor, not a med spa” / brand register

0 direct corpus instances · anticipated from product material
Surface form Cosmetic dermatology and plastics owners pushing back on what they perceive as discounting language, Groupon-style framing, or med-spa positioning. Hasn’t hit the war room corpus yet because cosmetic derm has zero calls in the dataset, but anticipated based on the broader sales motion.
Underlying concern Brand register. The prospect’s patient base associates the practice with premium medical positioning, and they don’t want a patient-facing tool that signals discounting. They’re not asking whether RepeatMD has memberships — they’re asking whether the word “rewards” will appear in their patients’ experience.
Response motion (provisional) Lead with the Sephora-style framing rather than the Starbucks-style framing. Memberships as a valuation multiplier, not as discounting. The app is white-labeled to the practice’s brand — the prospect’s logo, colors, copy register. Show the customization layer before the rewards layer. Worth coaching on this when a derm call lands.
Traps to avoid Don’t use the word “loyalty” in the opener with this prospect shape. Don’t lead with the rewards demo. Don’t cite med spa reference customers as social proof.

Economics

three objection shapes · the prospect is testing whether the math works for their practice

These objections show up after operational fit lands and pricing is on the table. The prospect is comparing the platform’s cost structure against what they’re already paying for and asking whether the swap math works. Specific numbers matter here — vague answers signal vague thinking and lose deals.

“Cherry / CareCredit gives us [X%] at [Y months]”

6 instances · formal pattern (Bill cleanest articulation)
Surface form Prospect quotes their current patient-financing fee structure from memory, often with specific term lengths and percentage points. Plastics carries this heaviest because the financing conversation drives high-ticket purchase decisions, but med spa and wellness also surface it regularly.
“Cherry gives us 2.9 percent at 24 months at zero.” Bill (Peninsula Plastic Surgery, paraphrased from corpus)
Underlying concern The prospect knows their current numbers and wants a head-to-head comparison, not a sales pitch. They want to know whether RepeatMD’s financing is genuinely better for their practice’s specific volume and patient mix, or whether the comparison is favorable only under certain conditions.
Response motion (the head-to-head) Flat-rate vs sliding-scale framing. Affirm is 3.99% flat regardless of term length or patient credit score. Cherry and CareCredit use sliding-scale rates that penalize the practice for the risk of lending. Approval rate is 81% (Affirm) vs typically 50–60% (Cherry/CareCredit). Credit line is $22,500 vs $7,500–$15,000 typical. Term length goes to 36 months. The defensible pitch is “flat-rate practice fee against variable-rate sliding scale,” not “our financing is better.” Bill’s call is the cleanest executive walkthrough of this comparison in the corpus.
Traps to avoid Don’t claim Affirm is universally cheaper — for very short terms with high-credit-score patients, Cherry’s zero-percent intro window is real and the rep should acknowledge it. Don’t skip past the approval-rate point — that’s where the head-to-head math actually breaks for high-volume practices. Don’t conflate the practice fee with the patient’s APR.

“I already do my own financing”

1 instance · singleton, distinct from no-financing objection (Maria)
Surface form Prospect runs in-house payment plans on expensive packages. Maria’s shape: charts half upfront, then charges per session for the remainder. Different from the no-financing objection because the prospect has already solved the lump-sum problem — they’ve just solved it via manual billing rather than a third-party tool.
“I do in finance, I do through myself. […] I chart like half of the package first and then keep charging them every session.” Maria (Rejuvenation Medical Aesthetics, Mar 5, 2026)
Underlying concern Whether the cost of switching (paying a 3.99% fee versus chasing per-session billing for free) is worth the operational relief. The prospect is doing time-cost math — their own time to track and bill manually versus the fee.
Response motion (the lump-sum reframe) The pitch wedge isn’t “you need financing” (they already have it). It’s “here’s a way to get the lump sum upfront so you stop chasing per-session billing.” The 3.99% Affirm fee is the cost of converting future-billing-headache into present-cash. Frame the operational time saved against the fee, not the fee against zero.
Traps to avoid Don’t dismiss the in-house arrangement. Don’t treat it as a no-financing objection. Don’t lead with the patient’s benefit (instant approval, soft credit) — the prospect’s patients are already paying them, the prospect’s concern is their own bookkeeping.

“Wait, who pays the convenience fee?”

3 instances · formal pattern (Marie strongest articulation)
Surface form Prospect confused about who pays the 2.5% in-app convenience fee — the practice or the patient. The confusion compounds when the financing fee (3.99% Affirm) gets layered on top. The prospect ends up unclear whether they’re paying 2.5% + 3.99% on every transaction, or one or the other, or neither.
Underlying concern Total cost of ownership clarity. The prospect isn’t pushing back on the fee itself — they’re trying to model the all-in cost of running the platform and need the fee structure unambiguous. Confused fee math means they can’t commit because they can’t defend the cost to a partner, CFO, or absent surgeon.
Response motion Clean disclosure. The 2.5% convenience fee is paid by the patient, not the practice, on every in-app purchase. The 2.79% + $0.30 card processing fee is paid by the practice when the patient pays with a card. The 3.99% + $0.30 Affirm financing fee is paid by the practice when the patient pays with Affirm. The patient pays one or the other (the card fee OR the Affirm fee), never both. Walk through a sample transaction end-to-end if needed.
Traps to avoid Don’t breeze past the fee structure assuming the prospect followed. Don’t conflate the patient-side and practice-side fees. Don’t bury the convenience fee at the end of the pricing slide — surface it explicitly.

Sales-pace shapes (not objections, but treated like them)

two shapes · structural constraints on close timing

These aren’t objections in the strict sense — the prospect isn’t pushing back on the platform. They’re structural constraints on how fast the deal can close. Treating them like objections (something to overcome) damages the deal. Treating them like pacing information (something to plan around) closes the deal at the right speed.

“I need to talk to my [husband / wife / partner / surgeon] first”

7 instances · load-bearing pattern across Pass 4
Surface form Prospect names a co-decider who isn’t on the call and explicitly defers final commitment until that conversation happens. Seven instances in Pass 4 alone: Sara’s business partner, Andy’s wife (medical side), April’s Robin, Marcela’s Chris (joined mid-call), Tiffany’s NP and surgeon, Kristin’s surgeon, Shannon’s Jessica (joined mid-call), Luigi joined with Dr. Ramos, Maria’s husband. The exact shape varies but the structural gate is consistent.
“Gotta go over with my wife who, you know, on the medical side, but could be interesting.” Andy (Primary Aesthetic Skin Care, Apr 24, 2026)
Underlying concern Almost never a hidden objection. The prospect has decided RepeatMD might be worth their attention and is following internal decision-making protocol. The risk isn’t that the co-decider will reject — it’s that the rep won’t equip the present prospect to sell the platform internally.
Response motion Ship the recording proactively, no exception. The present prospect needs the artifact to share. Schedule a specific calendar gate for the follow-up — not “text me when you’ve talked,” but a real time on a real day. Andy’s pending call is the only Pass 4 call without a calendar gate, and it’s also the most likely to drift. Offer to join a separate co-decider call if the present prospect wants that — many will decline (they want to do the internal sell themselves) but the offer matters. Surface what specifically will come up in the internal conversation and prepare the prospect to answer it.
Traps to avoid Don’t push card-on-call. Don’t treat the co-decider gate as a stall. Don’t skip the recording. Don’t let the follow-up be open-ended (“reach out when you’re ready”).

“I need a recording / time to review”

4+ instances · common across reflective buyers
Surface form Prospect explicitly asks for the call recording so they can re-review and formulate questions before committing. Sometimes paired with the co-decider gate, sometimes a standalone reflection signal. Sara, Shannon, Maria’s husband (asked by proxy), and others have surfaced this directly.
“Can you send me this recording so that I can sift back through it and formulate some questions. I’m sure that I’ll have.” Sara (Daytona MedSpa, May 6, 2026)
Underlying concern Buyer cognitive load. The 45-minute demo covered a lot of ground and the prospect wants time to re-encounter the platform without the rep’s pacing pressure. The risk isn’t reluctance — it’s that the rep won’t deliver the recording fast enough to keep momentum.
Response motion Ship within the hour. Set the expectation explicitly: “recording lands in your inbox in about ninety minutes once the system processes it.” If the rep is traveling (Alex was at the airport when Sara raised this), say so — the delivery delay needs to be transparent. Schedule the follow-up before ending the call — the recording is the artifact, the follow-up call is the close mechanism.
Traps to avoid Don’t promise the recording immediately and then take six hours to deliver. Don’t leave the follow-up open-ended. Don’t send the recording without a written follow-up note tying it to next steps.
Objections V1. Sources: 18 war room calls across Alex, Remy, Nellie, Taylor. Sorted by section, not by frequency — structural distinctiveness matters as much as recurrence. Pattern thresholds: 1 = surfacing, 2 = near-pattern, 3 = formal. Cards will refine as additional passes land. Cosmetic dermatology objections carry the heaviest evidence gap pending a derm call in the corpus.
